text:
Do people develop a ‚Äòcurated‚Äô music taste or does it form naturally?
I‚Äôve been thinking a lot about music taste and I don‚Äôt really understand how people actually develop a ‚Äúcurated‚Äù or defined one.

I listen to a pretty wide range of music; older stuff from the 50s onwards, soul/R&B like Etta James, jazz like Billie Holiday and Ella Fitzgerald, and rock. Some artists I come back to a lot are David Bowie, The Police, Smashing Pumpkins, Nirvana, and Linkin Park. Lately I‚Äôve been getting more into jazz!

But I don‚Äôt feel grounded in any of it. I can‚Äôt really point to a favorite artist, or a specific kind of rock I like, or what actually connects the music I enjoy.

Most of my listening feels kind of scattered. I usually hear a song, like it in the moment, add it to a playlist, and then move on, so I end up knowing a lot of individual songs but not really any artist deeply. Even with artists I do like, it‚Äôs mostly their more popular songs or tracks I‚Äôve randomly come across rather than exploring their work in depth. I‚Äôve listened to a few albums here and there, but they don‚Äôt always really fully stick with me.

I guess I‚Äôm trying to understand how people actually figure out what they like in a deeper sense. Is it normal to like music in this more ‚Äúfragmented‚Äù way, or does it usually become more specific and defined over time? I don‚Äôt really know what it means to say I listen to an artist when my experience of them is just a few songs here and there.

When I see people who are really into music, their taste feels so intentional and coherent. They know albums, eras, influences, and their music feels like it actually represents them. People can even say ‚Äúthis artist suits you‚Äù or ‚Äúthis is so your sound.‚Äù

How does that actually happen?
label: question
notes: User wonders whether music taste develops naturally or through deliberate exploration and feels disconnected from the music they enjoy despite listening to many genres.

text: Why is an artist's music often perceived as "cheapened" by having a predominantly teenage female fanbase? Why do people treat an artist's music as less valuable simply because it's popular among teenage girls? I've noticed that for many artists, the moment a predominantly teenage female fanbase becomes associated with them, people start dismissing the music itself, as though the audience somehow reduces the artistic value of the work. What I find particularly strange is the assumption that teenage girls liking something is evidence against its quality. Teenagers often experience emotions very intensely and tend to form deep personal connections with music... Why is "teenage girls like it" so often used as evidence that something is artistically inferior?
label: question
notes: Asking why music associated with teenage girls is often considered artistically inferior.

text: Opinions on Tate McRae's hit song 'Sports Car'. Considered to be her breakthrough hit apart from You Broke Me First or Greedy, so I'm curious where everyone stands. I think it's heavily carried by its production, but in the best way possible. The electropop and aggressive bass make the track addictive. The intentional whisper in the chorus helps it stand out. It also shows Tate in a different light, more confident and provocative. Overall, I think it's a strong pop track. What do you all think? Do you feel like Tate's label is pushing a more sexualized image, or does this feel like a natural evolution for her?
label: question
notes: Asking for opinions on Tate McRae's song, image change, and artistic direction.

text:
How long before Jack Antonoff and Jordan Fish Collab?
On paper, pairing mainstream pop king Jack Antonoff (Taylor Swift, Lana Del Rey, Bleachers) with ex-Bring Me The Horizon sound-design wizard Jordan Fish sounds completely unhinged. They operate in entirely different musical ecosystems, but under the hood, a collaboration actually makes perfect sense. Both are absolute production maximalists who specialize in creating massive, cinematic walls of sound.

Imagine merging Antonoff's warm, pulsing, retro 80s analog synths and organic, stomping percussion with Fish's biting digital synth leads, glitchy electronic beats, and complex, heavily layered vocal production. It would be an incredibly rich, high-energy soundscape that retains a raw, emotional heartbeat.

They wouldn't even need to form a band; just think of the absolute chaos they could cause as a co-producing duo. They could easily push a pop giant like Halsey, Olivia Rodrigo, or Billie Eilish into dark, heavy, industrial-pop territory, or pull a massive alternative band like Spiritbox, Bad Omens, or even BMTH themselves into pristine, stadium-sized pop focus.

With Jordan Fish currently operating as a free agent and Antonoff always hunting for unexpected textures to keep his sound fresh, this crossover isn't a total pipe dream. The gap between heavy alternative music and mainstream pop has never been smaller, and a collab between these two titans would absolutely break the industry. Would this be a masterpiece of modern sound design, or just an overproduced, chaotic mess?
label: argument
notes: Makes a case that a collaboration between Jack Antonoff and Jordan Fish would be creatively and commercially successful, providing multiple supporting reasons.

text:
Lianne La Havas deserves to be so much bigger than she is
Her music is so well-made, so well thought out. If it reached more people, I have no doubt they'd love it. The problem is it's just not reaching them.

Part of it might be the lack of music videos (she's got a very few). Part of it might be that she doesn't follow the current formula you know, selling a visual identity alongside the music, the way a lot of artists do right now. And yeah that's her choice. But it does mean she's not breaking through the way she deserves to.

She's just making great music. Check her out. She is really cool.

Edit: I feel like some of the replies are missing what I was actually getting at, so just to clarify. At no point was I saying I want her to be as famous as Dua Lipa, Sabrina Carpenter, Olivia Dean or any other big artists or that she ‚Äúshould‚Äù be on some kind of fame scale.

Her music is outstanding, and as a fan there‚Äôs a natural feeling of wanting more people to discover it simply because it feels like it deserves a wider audience. I also think there are a few reasons she might not be as widely discovered yet, which I mentioned earlier. Or perhaps I simply rate her music unusually highly.

Her visual identity is something I actually admire. She already has a very distinct style that suits her artistry. My mention of visual identity was only in the broader sense of how music tends to travel today, not as a criticism of her or her success (was a bit disheartened that it was taken that way).

She is already respected, successful, and has achieved things many artists never reach. So the idea that this was about demanding a higher level of fame takes my point in a direction I didn‚Äôt intend.

Out of interest, are there any artists you feel similarly about?
label: opinion
notes: Expresses the view that Lianne La Havas is an exceptional artist who deserves a wider audience and discusses possible reasons for her limited mainstream recognition.

text:
Anyone else here dislike solo acoustic singer performances?
Im talking about the busker types of performers where its just them singing while playing a guitar, whether be it originals or covers.

I get the essence of acoustic singers. Logisticswise, theyre the best pick (easy set up and pack up time, doesnt take up space, music isnt too jarring to bystanders) but to me, I just find it monotonous after 1-2 songs.

What appeals me to music is seeing how 2 or more people lock in with one another to produce something cool.

Yet however on the flip side, when I do go see an acoustic performer, I make sure its one I really really like.
label: question
notes: Asking whether others share a dislike for solo acoustic singer performances and discussing why they prefer music created by multiple musicians.

text:
What Have You Been Listening To? - Week of June 15, 2026
whyblt?
Each week a WHYBLT? thread will be posted, where we can talk about what music we‚Äôve been listening to. The recommended format is as follows.

Band/Album Name: A description of the band/album and what you find enjoyable/interesting/terrible/whatever about them/it. Try to really show what they‚Äôre about, what their sound is like, what artists they are influenced by/have influenced or some other means of describing their music.

Artist Name ‚Äì Song Name If you‚Äôd like to give a short description of the song then feel free

PLEASE INCLUDE YOUTUBE, SOUNDCLOUD, SPOTIFY, ETC LINKS! Recommendations for similar artists are preferable too.

This thread is meant to encourage sharing of music and promote discussion about artists. Any post that just puts up a youtube link or says ‚ÄúI've been listening to Radiohead; they are my favorite band.‚Äù will be removed. Make an effort to really talk about what you‚Äôve been listening to. Self-promotion is also not allowed.
label: question
notes: Weekly discussion thread inviting users to share and discuss the music they have been listening to recently.

text:
How did you "discover" your favorite bands?
One of my longtime favs is Los Lobos... I was stationed at Eielson AFB south of Fairbanks in the USAF in the mid-80s and there was a bar north of Fairbanks an hour or so called the Dog or the Fox which is/was a cool local establishment especially in the summer with the extended summer days.. House band was called The Flyers and during my first visit I heard Shakin', Shakin', Shakes which blew me away. I had to ask one of the band members but I became an instant Los Lobos fan with that song in the northern reaches of Alaska...
label: question
notes: Asking how people discovered their favorite bands.

text:
Your favorite musicians‚Äô favorite musicians
As a speculative exercise today I was trying to put together a list of 10 albums that I consider favorites, that I feel represent my tastes well, and that aren‚Äòt just the usual suspects praised constantly by everyone. (The idea was that these would be the choices I‚Äôd pick if were asked to do something like The Quietus‚Äô favorite records series, even though there‚Äôs no reason I would ever be picked for something like that; vain, I know!)

I won‚Äòt share my list here, but it struck me as I was narrowing down my choices how many artists that I probably would've picked five or ten years ago I was leaving off, and also how many of the artists I *was* picking were introduced to me through those earlier favorites, or at least were important influences on them. I left off Jeff Buckley but included Nina Simone and Leonard Cohen. I left off my teenage favorite Thrice but included Slowdive, a band I originally learned about through an interview with Thrice‚Äôs Dustin Kensrue. Almost everything on the list could be traced in this way to something else that used to be a cherished favorite of mine, until I started to get more interested in what had led there, which in turn gave me a new, even more cherished favorite.

I feel like this is a pretty typical trajectory for dedicated music fans, who are always looking to discover previously-unfamiliar things and to understand the precedents and lineages for the music they like. On the other hand, it seems a lot of people do just kind of settle on their favorites in their teens or twenties and don‚Äôt really branch out from there. And of course some ‚Äúdedicated music fans‚Äú are probably more interested in the present than the past, and are eager to discover new artists and sounds rather than dwelling on the old.

I guess if I were to shape all this into some discussion questions they would be: How much of your listening life do you spend investigating your favorites‚Äò favorites? How much of the music you love now did you find because it was an influence on someone else? And how many old favorites have become overshadowed for you by the things that inspired them?
label: question
notes: Asking how often people explore the influences of their favorite musicians and discover music through those connections.

text:
Is there a sense of what "reddit music taste" entails?
We've had some threads about how different types of websites, forums, magazines, and music critic fanbases will have tastes that lean towards certain directions, artists, and albums. I've read discussions about mu-core, rym-core, fantano-core, topster, the general ratings on 1001 albums, best-ever albums, etc. Plus magazines like Rolling Stone, Pitchfork, NME, SPIN, etc.

For several of these, there's a general sense of people leaning towards indie, alternative, and experimental works alongside popular canonical works.

But one category I don't quite understand is "Reddit Music Taste". I would see commenters talking about how a certain band or artist is really beloved by Reddit like Queen or David Bowie. Since they're just popular artists in general, it doesn't really tell me much.

My best guess on what reddit music taste might entail is a sort of strong memetic admiration for an artist. It's usually artists that strike a critical acclaim and commercial success sweet spot. You might be able to predict what comment is going to be upvoted right to the top.

Is there any consensus or sense of what reddit music even is? Have you encountered this type of comment?
label: question
notes: Asking whether there is a recognizable "Reddit music taste" and what characteristics define it.

text:
it amazes me how many people listen to music that challenges them and never give it another try instead of attempting to get into it and listen multiple times until it eventually clicks
when i look into reviews and takes that the general public give about albums that are experimental and challenging to get into, they will say that it is horrible instead of giving it another try.

usually, when i listen to a song that i don‚Äôt ‚Äúget‚Äù, i will, say, listen to another album by the same band/musician that is more accessible, listen to adjacent music that is more accessible, or just listen again. i look at these reviews and see people not letting music challenge them, and instead just return to their safe and comfortable taste.

i especially do what i have said i do when i see an album that most people (usually music nerds and people who are especially into music) enjoy, and i don‚Äôt enjoy it. i, personally, want to understand why people like the music they like, even if it is very experimental.

so, why do you think some people don‚Äôt have a want to step out of their comfort zone? what are your thoughts?
label: opinion
notes: Expresses the belief that listeners should give challenging or experimental music multiple chances before dismissing it.

text:
Why is an artist's music often perceived as "cheapened" by having a predominantly teenage female fanbase?
Why do people treat an artist's music as less valuable simply because it's popular among teenage girls?

I've noticed that for many artists, the moment a predominantly teenage female fanbase becomes associated with them, people start dismissing the music itself, as though the audience somehow reduces the artistic value of the work.

What I find particularly strange is the assumption that teenage girls liking something is evidence against its quality. Teenagers often experience emotions very intensely and tend to form deep personal connections with music. If a piece of music resonates strongly with a demographic that is actively seeking emotional expression, why is that treated as a sign that the music is shallow, rather than a sign that it has connected with its audience?

Why are teenage girls' preferences so often used as a metric for judging the worth of music?

(Before anyone wonders ‚Äî Yes, I am a 19 year old female. And no, I am neither a Swiftie, nor a K-pop fan.)

The funniest part of this thread is that a surprising number of people seem convinced I'm secretly trying to defend my own music taste.

I explicitly stated that I'm not a fan of those artists because I foresaw exactly this happening: a crowd of people rushing in to explain why the artists are bad, while completely sidestepping the point I was trying to discuss. What's especially ironic is that my own music taste is quite far removed from the mainstream pop music and fandoms that people keep assuming I'm talking about.

The reality is that my personal taste has very little to do with the question. Even if I listened exclusively to music that fell completely outside the stereotypes people associate with teenage girls, I'd still be asking the same thing:

Why is "teenage girls like it" so often used as evidence that something is artistically inferior?

At times it feels like people are arguing with a stereotype they've invented rather than the question I actually asked.
label: question
notes: Asking why music popular with teenage girls is often viewed as less valuable or artistically inferior.

text: I wonder if we're overestimating the impact of current popular music on music history I think we often overestimate the impact popular musicians have had since the 20th century. I mean, I can't name a single important musician from 500 years ago. The first one that comes to mind is Bach, and he's from the 17th century. Every time I see a list of the most influential popular musicians, it's a list with 20 names, and frankly, most people (I'm not talking about music lovers) wouldn't be able to name a single song by many of them. So, why do you think your favorite musician would transcend the others? What kind of music will people think of in the future when they think of the music of our century?
label: question
notes: Asking whether we overestimate the long-term influence of today's popular musicians and what music will be remembered in the future.

text:
Why do people not take the indie/rock genre seriously?
Indie/rock is by far my favourite genre but I feel like it has a very cult following and most people I talk to about it don't take it seriously or laugh it off as 'indie shit'. I don't know if this is just an experience I've had but it's really frustrating when they listen to hip hop and rap and won't give anything else the time of day like I would to other genres. Does this come down to immature music snobbery or is it an actual thing that people don't like?
label: question
notes: Asking why indie and rock music are sometimes dismissed and whether this is due to music preferences or social attitudes.

text:
Never Mind the Bollocks, Here's the Sex Pistols
For the longest time, I had never really given this album a shot. I had heard repeatedly that the Pistols didn't know how to play their instruments and they were more about the spectacle than substance. While this may have been true for Sid Vicious and Malcolm McLaren, I was surprised by how tight Paul Cook and Steve Jones are on the album. In fact, it turns out that Vicious is barely on the album at all and Steve Jones is the primary bassist on the album (Jones: "We tried as hard as possible not to let Vicious anywhere near the studio"). Combined with Johnny Rotten's iconic anti-singing that's just dripping with sarcasm, I get why this is considered one of the greatest punk albums of all time.

Everybody knows "Anarchy in the UK" and "God Save the Queen," each good songs in their own right, but deeper cuts like "No Feelings," "New York," and "EMI" also hold the album together with angry, aggressive tunes that still hold up. Also, while "Submission" isn't my favorite song from the album, I love the story about McLaren wanting the group to write a song to help promote his BDSM store and Rotten taking the title "Submission" and turning it into a song about a submarine on a mission.

It's a shame that McLaren prioritized controversy and publicity over the group's musical output, ultimately causing them to break up after releasing only one album. It makes you wonder what would have happened if the Pistols had a manager that protected the group rather than exploiting them. But then again, maybe it's the Pistols' reputation as "the most dangerous band in the world" that helped make the music as good as it is.
label: opinion
notes: Positive review of the Sex Pistols' album, arguing that its musical quality is often underestimated.

text:
Is David Paich the most unsung living songwriter in the world? (Writer or co-writer on Africa, Roseanna, Hold The Line, Lowdown, Lido Shuffle, Lady Love Me One More Time, Human Nature, The Girl Is Mine, Heal The World, We Are The World)
Unbelievable track record when you look at David Paich's wikipedia. Most people only mention the names 'Porcaro' and 'Lukather' when discussing Toto, as well, even when. discussing who worked with Michael jackson, so I thought it was an interesting premise: Maaybe Paich is the most unsung songwriter alive!

I mispelled his name a minute ago, a phantom 'T', so I have re-posted this, but check out this giants discography sometime. Not to mention he played keyboards on Katy Lied with Steely Dan on Bad Sneakers and Black Friday.
label: question
notes: Asking whether David Paich is one of the most overlooked songwriters despite his extensive list of famous songwriting credits.

text:
What‚Äôs a moment when music made you feel truly connected to other people?
Hi everyone,

I‚Äôve been thinking a lot about how music connects people.

I‚Äôm not only talking about concerts or big festivals, but also the small moments.

I really love music, and one of the things I enjoy most is not just listening alone, but sharing that feeling with other people. Because of that, I recently bought a DDJ-FLX4 and started learning DJing.

I‚Äôm still very new to it, but what excites me most is the idea of choosing the right song at the right moment and helping people enjoy music together.

So I‚Äôm curious:

When was a time music made you feel connected to someone else, or to a group of people?

I‚Äôd love to hear your stories.
label: question
notes: Asking people to share experiences where music helped them feel connected to others.

text:
Music taste and temperament
Have you noticed that people often seek out music that is the opposite of their temperament? Often, those who are typically calm enjoy high-energy, fast-paced tracks, while those with more restless or chaotic temperament tend to prefer slow, melodic music. Does this pattern extend to dancing as well?

Essentially what is the balance between a person's current mood and their overall natural disposition in their music choice?
label: question
notes: Asking whether music preferences are influenced more by a person's temperament or their current mood.

text:
Anyone find themselves kind of weirdly annoyed with RYM?
For years Rate your music used to be one of my favorite resources to find new music, but now it feels like any time I go on there I just can't find anything to listen to anymore. Like I used to look forward to seeing something new pop up in the yearly top 10, but now it just seems like it's all full of black sludgecum metal or shit that sounds like it was written by an AI trained purely on Bluesky.

Am I just a boomer now? Have I reached the point where your brain decides it wants to listen to the same shit over and over again instead of something new?
label: question
notes: Asking whether others feel frustrated with Rate Your Music and whether changing tastes come with age.

text: The custom charts are where it's at - exclude 'Metal' or include whatever you're looking for. Personally, any time I'm not really feeling what's new, I make a project for myself and do a deep dive into a scene from the past that seems interesting and it almost always leads to fun places.
label: opinion
notes: Suggests using custom RYM charts and exploring older music scenes as a better way to discover music.

text:
What happened to the relationship between pop stars and rap?
I just realized I can't remember the last time any of these new generation pop stars (Sabrina Carpenter, Olivia Rodrigo, Chappell Roan, Billie Eilish, Gracie Abrams, Harry Styles, Benson Boone, etc.) had a song with a rap verse. In the past these genres went along really well. Even if there aren't many huge new rappers, in the past sometimes they could use lesser known one like Colby O'Donis on Just Dance by Lady Gaga. And the lack of that plum features may explain part of the decline of new rappers, along with the other direction of pop songs not getting to blow up on a rap song. Yes it's also true that rappers also aren't using pop stars, but some of them with moderate/lower level success which is most of them the new ones days would surely do it if they can, and it makes sense to me why Kendrick or Drake aren't going to be asking Olivia Rodrigo to be on a song instead of some RnB singer.

Does it reflect the decline in rap that the pop stars no longer think it's worth it? Some of these female artists relationship to men it doesn't quite feel right putting one on it, but with a lot of female rappers emerging earlier this decade (Doja Cat, Meghan Thee Stallion, Ice Spice, etc.) there was plenty of opportunity for female collab. Dua Lipa did a song with Meghan and has a few remixes, but overall I think even her career is a few rap collabs short of what you would expect in part cause the big rappers haven't been there like Rihanna's time.
label: question
notes: Asking why collaborations between pop stars and rappers have become less common in recent years.

text: Rap's popularity in the mainstream has declined. Last year was the first time in 35 years there were no rap songs in the top 40. So pop stars dont include them as much because it isnt popular enough.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the decline of rap's mainstream popularity explains why pop stars collaborate with rappers less often.

text: Went to see the movie Daredevil and heard Evanescence for the first time and liked them. Which eventually led to me see a rec on the internet to try Within Temptation if you like Evanscence. That then opened the door to symphonic metal and all kinds of bands that get no airplay in the United States and I eventually listened to Nightwish, which is where my username comes from, though I actually hated Nightwish at first as I can't stand their original lead singers voice. They switched singers a a few years later and loved the albums with the 2nd singer. Then they switched singers again and I love most everything sung by their current singer and they are now my fav band.
label: opinion
notes: Shares a personal story about discovering favorite bands through recommendations and evolving music tastes over time.

text:
Insane Clown Posse is actually kind of underrated.
Insane Clown Posse might be the most hated music group in modern history. More than Nickelback, more than Imagine Dragons, more than Limp Bizkit, more than any of them. Unlike those 3, ICP NEVER had support from media, and their fans were designated a gang.

But even though musically the group is certainly not everyone's cup of tea, they are honestly a lot better than people give them credit for.

They get judged harshly because they're not "serious" rappers- what the hell do you expect from guys who dress up in clown makeup and spray Faygo over their audience? Illmatic Part 2?

But they are great storytellers and have a solid sense of humor. Some songs I recommend are:

"12" from Riddle Box. This song is a great concept song. It‚Äôs about a jury of 12 and a guy who was sentenced to death by them for a crime he didn‚Äôt commit. So he comes back to kill all 12 that condemned him.

"The Pendulum's Promise" from Bizaar. This song is about people judging others, and don't realize... they are also in many ways describing themselves.

"Crossing Thy Bridge" from The Wraith: Shangri La. This is borderline metal. It's about being horrified at seeing children die at a young age, and talking to God about wanting redemption.

"Halls Of Illusions" from The Great Milenko. Probably one of the closest things these guys have to a "hit". A great concept song about taking child abusers and woman beaters on a deadly ride representing a fun-house to reflect upon their actions, as they are shown the consequences of their ways as they pass through the ‚ÄúHalls of Illusions‚Äù.

If you want to check out those songs, do so and give ICP a chance. If you just blindly go by the "ICP sucks" mentality that you're supposed to think because Eminem famously dissed them and Miracles went viral... more power to you.

You're not gonna want to drink Faygo and paint your face up after listening to these tracks, but it could give you a solid look at the other side of the clowns.
label: argument
notes: Defends Insane Clown Posse as underrated by providing examples of their storytelling, themes, and musical strengths.

text: As a music fan, I respect their DIY ethic, and how they've built themselves a genuine following, and when I've heard them speak, they seemed like pretty funny, somewhat normal people. But the music itself, at least to me, is completely unlistenable. I'll give anything a fair shot, from Khanate to The Osmonds, and all stops in-between. But ICP, nah, pass.
label: opinion
notes: Respects ICP's work ethic and fanbase but personally finds their music unlistenable.

text: ICP and their juggalo fanbase has been reevaluated by critics and the mainstream in general in the last ten years or so. The general consensus has shifted from them being low brow culture vultures to inclusive outsiders. Even people who don't like their music admit that they are good people.
label: argument
notes: Argues that public and critical opinion of ICP has improved, citing a broader reevaluation of the group and its fanbase.

text: Yeah, I was unfairly dismissive of Juggalos until I got to know someone who's a huge ICP fan who's legitimately one of the best dudes I know. I really feel like an asshole honestly. It was a handful of their fans at my high school who gave me that bad impression and it really wasn't fair of me to think that way based on a handful of their fans.
label: opinion
notes: Reflects on changing a negative view of Juggalos after personally getting to know an ICP fan.

text:
Whether you like them or not, Geese are one of the most ambitious modern rock bands, period.
They‚Äôre carving a path that is completely their own in the modern rock landscape. Frankly most new bands that gain mainstream success to the same degree as Geese are nowhere near as original. They get a lot of comparisons to Radiohead, Led Zeppelin, Television, The Strokes and Pink Floyd, and while they do have a bit of each of these bands in their sound, they draw from their influences very tastefully and always deliver a sound that is their own. Anyone calling them unoriginal is showing just how little attention they‚Äôve paid while listening. I can see why their sound is divisive though. Not liking them for personal preference reasons is understandable. The band is weird. It took me a while to enjoy. But calling them not creative or untalented is just a weak criticism I think. I think they deserve the comparisons to the bands I mentioned as their music is comparably good to those classic groups. Coolest mainstream rock band since probably Queens of the Stone Age
label: argument
notes: Argues that Geese are one of the most original and ambitious modern rock bands, supporting the claim with comparisons and reasoning

text:
Underground concerts are MUCH better than mainstream ones.
I am not sure if this is the right sub to post this but I'll find someone to agree with me.

Last night I went to a concert with 5 really popular artists (trappers and some reggaeton) in my country, but I didn't feel too much energy like people were barely singing at all and mostly only the chorus, also no one jumping no one acc moving and having fun, some guys were there trying to get some girls and not even caring about the concert, it felt really empty overall. To be honest I was not in the front of the stage more of the middle and also I don't really listen to the artists only to one but I went with some friends, who also were not even singing at all like barely, I barely knew some lyrics from tiktok but I still tried to sing with all my heart.

Like 7 months ago I went to the concert of a underground band from my country, they had a tour through the entire country, they are like memphis rap and that type of stuff, and trust me their crowd was CRAZY compared to the other concert, with only like 50 people it felt much better than the other concert with over 5000. Before the main guys there were 2 other guys in their like "friend group" who got just as much attention but for the main guys... There was a moshpit for every single song, everyone was singing bar for bar and word for word, I wish I could relieve that kind of concert again, but they barely come to my city even if they were acc born here, wish they would show more love for us.

P.S. this is just a rant I had and I want to hear anyone who disagrees or agrees
label: opinion
notes: Believes underground concerts provide a more energetic and authentic experience than mainstream concerts.

text: I think there's a question to ask here - what's your definition of underground? Underground usually refers to concerts that aren't even really registered anywhere I believe, not just "bands that aren't massively popular". If the band tours the entire country, they're probably not what I would call underground.
label: opinion
notes: Questions the definition of "underground" and argues that a band touring nationally may not truly be underground.

text: Ye I get what you mean, some wouldn't call them underground but trust me they are, like they didn't have a venue or smth, the whole concert was on a teathre stage, like the people and everything on a stage, and for reference they have like 16k monthly while the most listened rap/trap etc artists have 500k so i'd say they're underground but everyone calls underground what they think is underground
label: opinion
notes: Defends the view that the band is underground and explains why, while acknowledging that people define the term differently.

text:
Olivia Rodrigo's New Album
I've been seeing a lot of praise for Olivia's new album with very little critique at all so i wanted to share ny thoughts because my view seems to be very different. I see many saying things such as "this album is good for what it is" but I do believe this album wasn't even that good for what it was supposed to be. The concept had a lot more potential, but jumps right from drop dead girl crush to stupid song and it really has too much sad ballads which really just sound the same (first time I'm saying this, I usually hate the "it sounds the same" argument being said just because an album is sonically cohesive, because I love cohesive albums but these genuinely sound... kind of the same.)

I feel that the amount of ballads and sad songs take away from the concept of trying to show the chapters of a relationship in different stages, because she was so into trying to incorporate the "sad" aspect of a love song that she failed to capture the highs almost completely.

I'm not saying the album is bad, i am saying the album is atleast mediocre and at most good but at the very baseline. I do genuinely believe "the cure" is honestly one of the best songs released in recent times not just for Olivia. "Drop dead" is pretty bland and I didn't get the praise when it was released, everyone acting as if it's an insane, extraordinary pop song.

The part where I truly lose my braincells is the praising of "maturity" on this album .. really? Maturity? There was a good deal of maturity in GUTS is what I will admit. I'm also a teenager but if anything it makes me more confused... did I really just see multiple people in their 30s, 40s, late 20s, whatever it is... say this album is mature? An album filled with angsty teenager songs like maggots for brains (shes a "sad shell of a woman when her baby is away"), my way, ("here's the part where the girl gets pissed, the girls is me, you're in my way now"), drop dead (stalked you on the Internet, kiss me and I might drop dead, it's feminine intuition), expectations and a bunch of basic teen sadgirl ballads (not meant to be a bad thing but people calling it mature is getting to my brain) like purple, what's wrong with me (he's whats wrong with her, wow), less (very predictable meaning, wish he loved her less if it means he wont leave her), but no apparently the critics think it's mature too so im thinking bias?

The thing is I'm not saying it has to be mature. I don't understand this entire "maturity" discourse, let her write how she wants, she's like what.. 23? But the fact is it is NOT mature at all. One of the most teenager albums ever released. With GUTS I could understand. It has lacy, making the bed, the grudge, pretty isn't pretty and vampire which are well written and cover themes through a good lens. Even SOUR was more mature with jealousy jealousy, brutal and 1 step forward 3 steps back. I feel like everyone is using the cure as the single example for this album being "mature."

I usually don't like cherrypicking lyrics from an album to act like the rest of the thing is bad but you can pick out a line that makes you go "Oh wow..." from probably almost every song on the album.

The aesthetics also feel very... it feels like she made a pinterest board and decided to run with that and force this new softer, feminine persona except it just doesn't feel natural.

And please don't get me started with all the rock references. Atleast the cure is a good song but I get it, you're so very cultured. You like weezer, courtney love, the cure and you know all the drinking games. Has it ever been more common for singers to create an irritating personality along with their new music? They've always done it but it used to feel like the music was a reflection of THEIR current persona rather than THEM trying to be this forced version of the MUSIC instead. It's even crazier once you realise the album isn't even giving alcohol or rock girl anyway. Atleast she nailed the pinterest aesthetics?
label: opinion
notes: Critical review of Olivia Rodrigo's album, arguing that it is overrated, repetitive, and less mature than many people claim.

text:
I agree with some points about the ballads (Honeybee, Purple, Begged and Cigarette Smoke haven't made a lasting impression on my brain and I've probably listened to the album 3-4 times now). But I also think calling it immature is a little unfair. Okay the mature remarks are maybe a little overplayed, but there is a maturity in the self-reflection that comes with the album as a whole.

Maggots for brains for example, my husband and I listened to it and laughed because when were apart, we often feel like shells of ourselves to a point... I almost always eat less healthy, have worse habits when he's away for work etc. But I think that codependency is something that can be a red flag or something you find endearing. I dislike the idea that using language like this is immature. Having fun with lyrics and over exaggerating fits the style of the song.

I think, like a lot of mainstream art, it's not perfection, but also not a bad album. And it's adored by fans, and people are relating to it, interpreting it differently, dissecting it - which I think is what art is for.
label: opinion
notes: Partially agrees with the criticism but argues that the album contains meaningful self-reflection and connects with listeners in different ways.

text:
I really don‚Äôt get the adulation. But then again this is what poptimism is aiming for. Now if you have a single criticism you‚Äôre hating on things that girls love. Just let people have fun. It‚Äôs not that serious.

I don‚Äôt know why but I find it kind of depressing that people are just so quick to latch onto the first thing shoveled upon them by social media or a streaming service. We have the world of music at our fingertips but it feels like people just settle for this. And I guess this is fine but it‚Äôs also just pretty bland and unimaginative.

I listened to it yesterday and actually forgot I had it on until the song my way came on and I actually thought it was energetic and catchy but so much of it just felt like bland flavorless oatmeal.
label: opinion
notes: Expresses a negative view of the album, arguing that it is overpraised, unoriginal, and largely forgettable.

text:
Old man take: I like Olivia Rodrigo for what she is - she‚Äôs a very talented performer - but she‚Äôs a Disney child star making corporate music.

She may make overtures toward authenticity or garnish her music with references that signal street cred but at the end of the day, it‚Äôs pop. It‚Äôs never going to be particularly challenging or have a truly specific voice. It‚Äôs manufactured for mass appeal, which means that - at its best - it‚Äôs going to have simple, universal themes and memorable hooks‚Ä¶ and that‚Äôs pretty much it.

That‚Äôs not a dig on pop - in its own way, making pop music with broad appeal is even harder than making poetic indie rock for art school dropouts - but because it is made to target as many people as possible, the pintrest board aesthetic is just part of the bargain.

You might just be outgrowing it.
label: opinion
notes: Argues that Olivia Rodrigo is a talented performer making mainstream pop music designed for broad appeal rather than artistic depth.

text:
Do people develop a ‚Äòcurated‚Äô music taste or does it form naturally?
I‚Äôve been thinking a lot about music taste and I don‚Äôt really understand how people actually develop a ‚Äúcurated‚Äù or defined one.

I listen to a pretty wide range of music; older stuff from the 50s onwards, soul/R&B like Etta James, jazz like Billie Holiday and Ella Fitzgerald, and rock. Some artists I come back to a lot are David Bowie, The Police, Smashing Pumpkins, Nirvana, and Linkin Park. Lately I‚Äôve been getting more into jazz!

But I don‚Äôt feel grounded in any of it. I can‚Äôt really point to a favorite artist, or a specific kind of rock I like, or what actually connects the music I enjoy.

Most of my listening feels kind of scattered. I usually hear a song, like it in the moment, add it to a playlist, and then move on, so I end up knowing a lot of individual songs but not really any artist deeply. Even with artists I do like, it‚Äôs mostly their more popular songs or tracks I‚Äôve randomly come across rather than exploring their work in depth. I‚Äôve listened to a few albums here and there, but they don‚Äôt always really fully stick with me.

I guess I‚Äôm trying to understand how people actually figure out what they like in a deeper sense. Is it normal to like music in this more ‚Äúfragmented‚Äù way, or does it usually become more specific and defined over time? I don‚Äôt really know what it means to say I listen to an artist when my experience of them is just a few songs here and there.

When I see people who are really into music, their taste feels so intentional and coherent. They know albums, eras, influences, and their music feels like it actually represents them. People can even say ‚Äúthis artist suits you‚Äù or ‚Äúthis is so your sound.‚Äù

How does that actually happen?
label: question
notes: Asking whether music taste develops naturally over time or through deliberate exploration and curation.

text:
Listening to full albums is key. If you‚Äôre only listening to what you like you don‚Äôt have context for why you like it, and listening through albums that have songs you like and songs you don‚Äôt is how you develop your own taste. Being able to articulate why you do or do not like a song or genre or artist develops over time and listening to a lot of music.

Deep diving is useful, but it not for everyone. Not everyone needs to have a super developed taste for what they like, but I think if you‚Äôre looking to deepen your understanding of why you like what you like you just need to listen to more music. Try picking a few of the songs on your playlists and exploring that artist‚Äôs other work, and see who else is similar to them and exploring some of that. It doesn‚Äôt need to be regimented, it should be fun!
label: opinion
notes: Suggests that listening to full albums and exploring related artists helps develop a deeper and more defined music taste.

text:
I also find I generally need to listen to an album a few time for it to really set in. Some albums are coherent works. Some are just a spattering of songs. Many are 2/3 good songs and a bunch of filler. It might help to start with some highly rated ones. It's great when you know there's a song transition coming up that gets you hyped up. (Beastie Boys: Ill Communication into Sabotage, Led Zeppelin Heartbreaker into Livin' Lovin' Maid). There are often songs that on their own aren't that great. But the role they play in the album, as a breather, as a transition completely change them.

You should at least give nevermind like 4-5 play throughs over the next month. Or pick a Bowie album (I don't know Bowie well enough to help but I do know you want to choose wisely, he's had some stinkers).

I'll also randomly recommend "white Blood Cells" by the White Stripes. Bluesy rock and a lot of those songs hit differently than they do stand alone. Also lots of great songs that were never really singles.
label: opinion
notes: Recommends listening to albums multiple times and exploring complete albums to better appreciate music and develop taste.

text:
First, there no ‚Äúnormal‚Äù way to enjoy music. Listening to what you like and keeping an appetite to listen to new stuff is a great approach.

Personally, I‚Äôve gone through different ‚Äúeras‚Äù of music listening as I‚Äôve gotten older. I‚Äôve always listened to a variety of things, but middle school was definitely pop punk heavy, high school was indie rock, college was alternative, folk, and electronic. I‚Äôm now in my 30s and my taste spans all of those genres (and more).

I‚Äôve also always listened to full albums. I enjoy listening to an album from start to finish to really understand it.

As I look back, I‚Äôve spent a lot of time with different genres and artists and now I‚Äôm able to kind of piece things together in maybe more ‚Äúcurated‚Äù ways if I‚Äôm recommending stuff to others, putting together a playlist, or just choosing some music to play in the car while driving with others.

I will say, I‚Äôve also been pretty intentional the last handful of years to really learn more about music history and to seek out artists I haven‚Äôt typically listened to.
label: opinion
notes: Explains that music taste develops differently for everyone and shares a personal experience of how listening habits evolved over time.

text:
For me, it is all about life experience and relationships

I developed my love of rock and metal through shared experiences with friends, going to concerts, talking about albums, buying CDs, playing them while reading liner notes, and working at the music store. Debating whether Soundgarden, Pearl Jam, or Alice in Chains was better with my friends while trying to find the next most obscure good band was basically a hobby. Ozzfest and Lolla in the 90s were rites of passage.

I developed my love of outlaw country because my dad would play it growing up, on the radio and with his guitar. I think of riding in my dad's car, and Willie Nelson pops in my head.

I developed my love of EDM when I went back to college and was hanging with people 10 years younger than me. Every road trip seemed to have Calvin Harris in the background.

When my dad got sick, I would practice songs on guitar to play and sing for him and my mom. I went back deep into country and singer/songwriter stuff I could play/sing by myself on acoustic. I'll always remember playing Jason Isbell and learning to sing Sara Bareilles a full step+ down just so I could sing it for my parents.

I backdoored into pop when one of my favorite rock bands opened for Halsey. I did a Halsey deep dive, and now Halsey is one of my top 5 favorite artists of all time. Whenever I'd find a Halsey song I'd like, I'd bring it to my wife like a kid. "Honey, listen to this!" She doesn't like music like I do, but sharing that with her deepened my appreciation for the music itself. Liking Halsey opened me up to explore other pop, and find other favorite artists like Lorde and Olivia Rodrigo.

K-pop felt kind of accidental, but it coincided with the K-Pop Demon Hunters release. I was on a flight back from Vegas and googled "biggest K-pop artists" and listened to Blackpink Essentials, and was like, holy shit, this is GOOD. I have an early 20s employee who is huge into K-pop, so now we talk about trending releases and share recommendations. It's fun af. The coolest thing about K-pop is that the release schedule is so intense that there's always something new and exciting to try as soon as you get into it. Now there are like 6 groups I really enjoy, and they're always coming out with something to keep me engaged.

At the end of all that, I can talk about any of those genres with some degree of depth. But I can't say any of it was ever deliberate- I just went where my interest pulled me.
label: opinion
notes: Shares how personal experiences, relationships, and different stages of life shaped their music taste across multiple genres.

text:
You are your own curator.

I know you‚Äôre thinking of curation as specialized, focused discipline. And when it comes to museum or public collections, that true. And yes, we‚Äôve bled that sense into the private sphere ‚Äî you might refer to a friend who has a well curated collection of books, music, wine, etc.

But you as the curator of your own collection get to set your own rules! You aren‚Äôt beholden to a mission statement that says you need to have a collection representing the course of musical evolution in Jamaica between 1964 and 1976. You aren‚Äôt tasked with highlighting any genre, era, country, or anything.

To make it plain: you are not building a collection for me or anyone who isn‚Äôt you. I definitely won‚Äôt like some music you like, and you definitely won‚Äôt like some music I like. That‚Äôs ok.
label: opinion
notes: Argues that people curate their own music taste naturally and should not feel pressured to fit a specific definition of a "curated" collection.

text:
As a millenial, I feel like the limitations we grew up with was a breeding ground for curation. If you are getting your tastes from the algorithms it's a process that takes much less research and work.

Back in my day we used to have to buy songs or CDs or whatever, so we would build these much smaller collections and really listen to them (to get our money's worth).

I used to work in my college's music library and check out 5 CDs at a time and rip them into my itunes library. I would check out all kinds of music that was absolutely NOT trendy. It was fun.
label: opinion
notes: Believes that older limitations in accessing music encouraged deeper exploration and more intentional music curation.

text: It‚Äôs ok that you don‚Äôt get really into specific artists. For me what happens is often this: I hear a song I like. I listen to it again and realize I don‚Äôt just like it, I REALLY like it. So I listen to the artists other popular songs and the album that song came from. Sometimes they don‚Äôt hit as well as that song did, but sometimes they do. So I listen to that album until I wear it out and then do the same to their other albums, learning things about the band or singer along the way. It‚Äôs interesting to me that some people have never experienced this. But not anything wrong with you necessarily.
label: opinion
notes: Shares a personal approach to discovering artists by exploring albums after finding a song they really enjoy.

text:
I'm usually in a specific mood that vibes with the music I hear somewhere. I save the song name on my phone and look up the artist, cause I want to know if he makes more of what I like or find interesting. If I like 2 songs I will buy his album. Why? Because I want to understand why the artist does what he/she does. I want to get to know the artist through his music. Hits are often not the most authentic songs. I want to hear authenticity. I like weird songs that I have to listen to 5 times before they click with me. In a way I train my ears and brain to understand the artist. I also buy the albums because I want to financially support the artists. Cover arts often play a role too. In this process I find out artists and even genres that are more rewarding then others.

Good example for this is Steven Wilson and the genre ProgRock/Progmetal. In this music there are many tiny details that I can rediscover while listening over and over, because it's complex compositions, complex sounddesign and interesting rhytm patterns, effects etc.. That's why I cannot make myself listen to hiphop, autotuned pop or boring techno music.
label: opinion
notes: Describes a personal process of exploring artists through albums and values authenticity, complexity, and repeated listening.

text: I don't really think there is any point in trying to curate a music taste to be a specific way. For me it all kind of just came naturally by just finding a song I like and then listening to the album it's from. If I like the album, I might check out other albums by the same artist, or I might not, maybe I'll just listen to something completely different. I think it's fine, and honestly probably even good, to have a "fragmented" music taste like that, since then you'll end up being exposed to so many different sounds and styles and musical traditions than you would have if you only focused on curating a specific style of music taste. For me at least the joy in listening to music comes from always trying to find sounds I've never heard before, which I suppose does lead to me having a more fragmented music taste. Honestly I'd just tell you to not worry about it too much, just listen to whatever you feel like listening to. If you want to listen to jazz today and black metal tomorrow then why not just do that?
label: opinion
notes: Believes music taste develops naturally through exploration and that having a broad, fragmented taste can be beneficial.

text: Listening to full albums is a really great way to find what you like. Like- do a Bowie discography listen over the course of however much time you have. Could be months, could be a year or more. Find musicians you do like and look at their influences. If you listen to newer music, see if the song sampled or interpolated another. If you like an album, look up what genres it‚Äôs being described as, then look up albums are also described with those terms. Look up the record labels those people are under. Listen to scores of movies or video games or tv shows and look up what those musicians were inspired by (ex: I like Silent Hill‚Äôs score‚Äî they were inspired by Nine Inch Nails, Twin Peaks score , etc). Listen to the worst albums of all time, listen to random forgotten songs from the top 40 decades ago, or UK charts or charts anywhere but where you‚Äôre from. If you have a record store near you, write down some names and listen to them later (and buy if you like it). All of that is some of how I started listening to a pretty varied, but ‚ÄúMe‚Äù music, anyway
label: opinion
notes: Believes music taste develops naturally through exploration and that having a broad, fragmented taste can be beneficial.

text:
I listen to a pretty wide range of music; older stuff from the 50s onwards, soul/R&B like Etta James, jazz like Billie Holiday and Ella Fitzgerald, and rock. Some artists I come back to a lot are David Bowie, The Police, Smashing Pumpkins, Nirvana, and Linkin Park. Lately I‚Äôve been getting more into jazz!

But I don‚Äôt feel grounded in any of it. I can‚Äôt really point to a favorite artist, or a specific kind of rock I like, or what actually connects the music I enjoy.

So I guess my way of listening to music would be to seek that grounding in the style of music that I like. For example, if your taste were mine, and I wanted to understand more about Nirvana, I might dig deeper into the Seattle sound that birthed them, and explore similar artists such as Mudhoney, Melvins and Green River.

For David Bowie, who has so many distinct 'periods' in his career, I would branch out and explore his collaborators and contemporaries such as T. Rex, Brian Eno, Kraftwerk, Iggy Pop etc. and in doing so, develop a better understanding of why his music was so special in the context(s) it was created.

It is fun to find the line between popular genres of music across various eras. I got really into 70's disco many years ago and it led me to collecting a huge number of obscure italo disco and electro-funk records. Some acts like Daft Punk wear their influences on their sleeves and can bring you into a whole world of international pop music you were previously unfamiliar with.

I'm not sure if this really addresses your question as I don't think I consciously "curate" my taste to appear coherent, I am simply genuinely interested in the influences that shape the music I like and it leads to me discovering so many deeper musical cuts.
label: opinion
notes: Suggests exploring related artists, influences, and musical movements to gain a deeper understanding of the music you enjoy.

text:
I think it's a combination of them both. Personally, my taste in music has shifted, expanded and evolved over the years. I love genres of music now that I didn't care about 10+ years ago; my taste in music just developed upon being exposed to different sounds and just naturally gravitating towards them the more I heard. Sometimes our ears do require an adjustment period before we can actually come around to loving something.

My music library is all over the place in between garage rock, drum and bass, trance, reggaeton, jazz, heavy metal, R&B, Celtic, film score, synthpop, etc. Some genres I gravitated towards naturally and others I did have to become accustomed to the sound to the point where I now do love it. For how sporadic my library is, it's definitely intentional and of a solid interest as it's full of full-length albums. I don't listen to or make playlists. The closest I get to that is listening to compilations or film/television soundtracks. Not only do I love the genres, but I love individual artists in each.
label: opinion
notes: Believes music taste develops through both natural exposure and intentional exploration, evolving over time as people encounter new genres and artists.

text:
You really have to put the effort into it in order to really understand one particular artist. If you just know their biggest hits, you are getting only 10% of the big picture. It's like an iceberg. The top of the iceberg is what the radio plays or what friends will recommend, but the majority of the iceberg is what you will discover when you dive deep and listen to every album by a specific artist. Obviously this is not easy to do for most people because it's quite time consuming. I actually just made a post about this yesterday in here.

As for me personally, I'm in my 50's so I've definitely had the time to deep dive into artists that I want to get to know better, however most of my life I pretty much only knew what was on the radio and what me and my friends listened to. So it's a combination of it happening naturally and me taking control over what my music taste is. The more music you hear, the more you will be drawn to a certain type unless you're the kind of person that really is scattered and can like almost anything with no obvious preferences.
label: opinion
notes: Believes that understanding an artist requires deep exploration beyond their popular songs and that music taste develops through both experience and intentional effort.

text: Offhand, I'd have to say deliberate listening. Listening to an album, or building specific playlists and listening to them. If one is primarily listening to an algorithm-driven source, there may be a detachment because you're not really investing anything into the act of listening. This would have held true for people who primarily listened to the radio, but even in the old days of actual DJs choosing the playlist, there would still be intent behind it (versus now where the primary goal isn't musical interest, but market dominance). You even use the word yourself, "intentional and coherent", if you're just clicking an icon on a screen because you like a song, that's a pretty surface-level engagement.
label: opinion
notes: Argues that intentional listening, such as exploring albums and creating playlists, leads to a deeper connection with music than algorithm-driven listening.

text: What do you think it would mean to feel ‚Äúgrounded‚Äù? There are people whose personalities seem sort of predicated on a rather narrow taste in musical genres- we all know people who only listen to country or hip hop and consider it a cultural marker. But I presume you‚Äôre not talking about that. Most people who enjoy music for what it is tend to have fairly porous tastes, where the more you listen to what you like, the more interested you become in adjacent and related genres. You might be ‚Äúgrounded‚Äù in what you listened to early in life, or songs or artists you associate with exciting or difficult or memorable times in your life. Such associations are sometimes unbreakable, and give the music significance beyond its content or themes. But I presume you already understand those things, and I‚Äôm not sure what else you might be referring to.
label: question
notes: Asks what it means to feel "grounded" in music taste and explores how personal experiences shape musical preferences.

text: Prioritizing listening to whole albums is a good start, but also you're allowed to enjoy music how you want. If it doesn't come to you naturally, then it's always gonna come across as forced. Mind you there's already enough pretentious asshats in online music communities trying to one up each other with how profound, refined, and diverse their taste is. Being on reddit you only get to see the most obsessive and nerdy representatives of every hobby and you might get the impression you have to be like that to be a true fan. I enjoy movies, but I'm not a cinephile. I enjoy coffee, but I'm not a nerd about it. Being just kinda into things is okay and pretty cool, actually.
label: opinion
notes: Believes people should enjoy music in their own way and not feel pressured to develop highly curated or obsessive music tastes.

text:
Listen and follow your instincts. You will find you go through various phases. At least I have.

A few summers ago, I couldn't get enough of both dark wave and reggae/dub.

This year its mostly jazz fusion, jazz funk and ambient.

I still have old favorites, of course, but those are my enthusiasms right now. One process that's been helpful to me is I add any album I hear about that I might want to explore to my Amazon Music account. At this point, I have over 20,000 songs. When I don't know what to listen to, I click on all songs and play that on shuffle. When something really catches my ear, then I explore that artist in more depth.
label: opinion
notes: Encourages following personal interests in music and describes how musical preferences can change over time through exploration.

text:
For starters, buy physical copies of music you like. Use services to test listen, but at some point, support the artists. Making the investment will lead to a stronger connection. Also, you own it forever. I can draw a line from Bechet to Lightening Hopkins to The Sonics to The Butthole Surfers to Six Fingered Satellite because I own and understand all that music. If I had just heard a song or two in passing, I would not be able to make the same connections.

Also, owning full albums will help you learn what you don‚Äôt like. What you don‚Äôt like is as important as what you do. I can talk about a band like the Zombies by saying they‚Äôre a Merseybeat / psyche crossover band, but they‚Äôre not as cheesy as most. I love me some Merseybeat, but man a lot of it was horribly written. So when you come across a band who avoids the trappings that turn you off, you recognize the value.
label: opinion
notes: Argues that owning full albums and supporting artists helps build a deeper understanding of music and personal taste.

text: I think it's to do with physical media v streaming. Although I use streaming to check out new (and new to me) stuff, my listening for pleasure is vinyl. My vinyl listening is curated in the sense that I'd rather listen to a second choice album by a band I like than that one album I like from a band that most of the time I don't like. I also consciously think about my taste, and what connects the music I like. For example, I like conversational lyrics, so explore that and see where it takes me. I think this goes back to the days when your record collection was a public statement of your taste. Even if no one is flicking through my records, I still keep something of that old idea. The other thing about physical media is you're more invested in it, you listen to something several times because you own it, and the more you listen to it the more it grows on you. To some extent you're choosing what you want to like, rather than stumbling upon random albums this way and that.
label: opinion
notes: Believes physical media encourages more intentional listening and helps people develop a clearer understanding of their music taste.

text: Way back when they had a social media platform called my space. I remember meeting a girl on my space who listened to pagan rock . I asked what about speed metal ? She said - just pegan rock . I said no punk ? Art damage ? Industrial? She says no - just pegan rock
label: opinion
notes: Shares an anecdote about someone whose music taste was focused almost entirely on a single genre.

text: My theory is that people that identify closely with a particular kind of music came into contact with that particular sound at either a crucial time of their lives. That crucial time could be anything from a coming of age awareness moment as a kid or some moment of first tasted freedom. It could be a traumatic or intense context and the music eased the suffering in a way. Music is the soundtrack to our existence. We are curated by the events of life. The soundtrack to those particular moments become touchstone sounds. Of course there are other reasons and paths to particularity. The main thing is to enjoy it.
label: opinion
notes: Suggests that strong music preferences are often shaped by important life experiences and emotional connections.

text:
Tips and tricks to building playlists?
I got hella songs and listen to a lot of rap, R&B, alternative, melodic stuff, but when it comes to actually making playlists I kinda struggle. Not with finding songs, more with making the playlist feel like it has a theme or vibe instead of just being a bunch of songs thrown together.

What are some of your favorite playlist themes or concepts you‚Äôve made? Could be something broad enough for 100+ songs or something super specific
Let me know what your guys process is
label: question
notes: Asking for advice on creating playlists with a clear theme, mood, or overall vibe.

text: I don't really do playlists for personal use. If I'm not listening to an album, I'll just pick the next song I want to hear as the current one is playing. Is playlist culture a generational thing? A genre thing?
label: question
notes: Asking whether playlist culture is influenced by generation, genre, or personal listening habits.

text:
My method for a while has been just shuffling a big playlist.
I‚Äôll usually play the song I really want to hear in the moment and just skip the ones I don‚Äôt want to hear often that
I want to organize my music so I don‚Äôt have to do that often I have 2 playlist already one with love songs and one that give confidence now the topics are more specific then just that but when I want to feel that way I automatically have them there and I‚Äôll play the song I want to
label: opinion
notes: Shares a personal method for organizing music using playlists based on moods and frequently listening to a large shuffled playlist.

text:
I make mostly concept playlists (like a concept album). One of my favorites is titled "this house is haunted" and it's all songs about unpleasant things either taking place in a house or using a house as a metaphor.

I make loads of temp playlists. If I hear a song and think it would be interesting played next to a different song, I make a playlist. If I think a song would be interesting in a playlist but not sure exactly how, I add it to my "songs for future playlists". If I have an idea for a playlist, I'll make one before I even know what songs I'm gonna add (currently working on a zodiac playlist where there's one song for each sign). I have many ongoing playlist ideas, some of which will probably go nowhere and get deleted eventually.
label: opinion
notes: Describes a personal approach to playlist creation using themes, concepts, and experimental song combinations.

text:
honestly biggest thing that helped me was stop trying to make every playlist "mood coherent" and just pick one specific scenario, like "driving home at 1am" instead of "chill vibes." way easier to know what fits and what doesn't when it's that specific

also order matters more than ppl think, i don't just dump songs in randomly, i ease in, build up, then taper off at the end so it feels like it goes somewhere instead of just shuffling

for the big 100+ song ones i'm pretty loose on genre as long as the energy matches, for the tiny specific ones i'm way more ruthless about kicking stuff out even if i like the song

also just go back and prune old playlists every once in a while, stuff you added during a phase doesn't always hold up with the rest
label: opinion
notes: Recommends building playlists around specific situations, carefully ordering songs, and regularly removing tracks that no longer fiit.

text:
I use the Smarter Playlists app (Spotify plugin) that allows me to take the standard Spotify playlists and combine them/augment them as required.

I run these once a month to generate new playlists each month. I keep an archive list (as part of the process) against which to compare to ensure that there are no previously included songs in the new playlists.

If I hear songs I like I add them to new playlists. The monthly generated ones are discarded once I've listened to them (although the archive file remains for future use).

Edit: in terms of your issue about not just throwing songs together, you can also do the above by picking particular genres/playlists for your playlist sources and the app allows you to sample from these based on pretty detailed parameters (I'm sure, for example, bpm can be used but I have not tried this).
label: opinion
notes: Shares a method for generating and organizing playlists using Spotify tools and personalized filtering rules.

text:
I go to a lot of live shows and I make playlists out of the set lists from my favorite performances. I also choose one song from every performer I see over the year and put those into a playlist.

Before I was streaming music and was managing my library on a desktop computer I added tags with random information about where they‚Äôre from, what language, etc. Then I‚Äôd make smart playlists that would automatically pull from my library so I would do a playlist from a single country, state, city, songs in Spanish, stuff like that.
label: opinion
notes: Describes personal playlist-building methods based on live concerts, locations, languages, and music library tags.

text:
I usually theme my playlists to a vibe based on production. Then always choose the opening track to be something I would genuinely replay the most. Sometimes I curate them to flow together well but that takes a lot of time.

To find similar songs of the same vibe for a playlist, for Spotify specifically I like the recommended songs section under the playlist as you‚Äôre constructing it. Then the song radios and artist radios now have the non-personalized feature which works sometimes. I‚Äôve discovered tons of songs artists through those!
label: opinion
notes: Shares a playlist-building strategy based on production style, song flow, and discovering similar tracks through Spotify recommendations.

text: Almost all of my playlists are mood or activity instead of genre. Rainy Day, Boogiedown, Walk on the Beach, Roadtrip, Sunday Morning Hangover, Sexy Time, Campfire etc. I also like to keep track of new things I discover reach year so a 2026 list is active now but I don‚Äôt really listen to it as much as reference it. I guess I have others like that. Protest is one that I scroll through for an appropriate one but I don‚Äôt play the whole thing unless the world is looking like it wants to burn down
label: opinion
notes: Describes organizing playlists around moods, activities, and personal experiences rather than music genres.

text: Someone else touched on it, but what's helped me is following the old, unwritten, mixtape rules - its gotta have a theme, it's gotta flow, you gotta start with a punch but not to much 'cause you don't want to blow your load right out the gate. Refine your ruleset around what you're working on and then stick to your limits. I know it antithetical in our current age with instant access to everything, but give yourself absolute sideboards and it starts getting a lot easier.
label: opinion
notes: Recommends using themes, flow, and self-imposed rules when creating playlists to make them more cohesive.

text:

text:
Temple Guard - Citadel in Flames https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=n49ON-biW2M. This is amazing to anyone that liked 90s metallic hardcore (Earth Crisis, Integrity...). Angry Vegan SXE HC though you don't have to be any of these things to appreciate it. Need more of this. Honestly, this is my favorite new album this year.

Swingin' Utters - Five Lessons Learned. This is solid East Bay punk with great production. Some of these songs belong on the radio and there is a definite British Invasion influence. This Bastard's Life is the Standout https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5dMvbVSy_k0

NOFX - Punk in Drublic. I don't know anyone in my circle that hasn't heard this too many times, but we can't deny this is one of the most important skate punk album from the 90s. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uSJznJPLamA

Blacklisted - So, You Are a Magician https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SlDAJWkZ_bk Hardcore Punk from Philly with a rock edge to it. This is a fun one for people that like a more old school hardcore sound.

Buddy Holly - s/t https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GMezwtB1oCU (Every day). I've heard Buddy Holly but never bothered to listen to an album of his. For a 1958 record this is incredible. We truly lost a genius too early. I wonder how he would have evolved with the 1960s.

New Order - Power, Corruption & Lies https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hZXxmhok1AU My 15 year old daughter liked this one. It's a classic. It's hard to describe New Order's dance/post punk sound but when they are firing on all cylinders it is perfect. I actually like them better than Joy Division, but I'll probably get killed for saying this.

Misfits - Earth A.D. - I'm a simple man. I enjoy hardcore punk and this is the Misfits HC album. Why is it HC? Because Robo was too fast on the drums. Death Comes Ripping is one of my faves for those that only Die Die Die My Darling. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72CYcUIj7P8. Are the lyrics stupid? Yes. Is the guitar work not that impressive? text:
New artist & Pitch Correction
Okay so I‚Äôm a huge fan of music. & lately I‚Äôm so unmoved by what been put out lately.

Chloe new album that was produced by Timbaland disappointed me although it‚Äôs better than her solo projects out in the past.

I was expecting this album to be framed similar to the late 90s/ early 2000‚Äôs. Raw vocals, bridges, real instruments etc! But no it‚Äôs just sounds like Timbaland‚Äôs same outdated sound.

And this post is in now way to bash either artist but I truly want more from Chloe she‚Äôs sooo talented. She‚Äôs literally the IT girl. Vocals, dance skills, actress‚Ä¶ the list goes on.

But Idk I think the over pitch correction throws me off and doesn‚Äôt fully allow me to enjoy new music.

Now Durand Benarr‚Äôs new album was a 10/10 for me. It had those 90s/00s elements while still being with the times and not overly copying it.

What do yall think? I‚Äôm not even able to enjoy the older artists live vocals cause everyone mic is corrected.

Bring back raw vocals pls!!
label: opinion

text:
Do all popular artists chase fame? Did fame ever happen to any artists who didn‚Äôt want it?
Yeah, I‚Äôm just curious whether all the popular artists of recent decades‚Ä¶ U2, Radiohead, Coldplay, Keane, Travis, Kendrick, DMX, Aerosmith, RHCP, etc etc etc etc‚Ä¶. actively chase fame, or whether it‚Äôs purely for the love of music and a burning desire to get your stuff out for the world to hear? Are there any artists who very much didn‚Äôt care for fame but it ended up coming their way anyways? I wonder whether certain artists and genres are content to be ‚Äúlower on the tadpole‚Äù, signed to some indie label and doing small regional tours, just happy to sustain themselves doing what they love- or if even they given the chance would want to make it big? I wonder what quality it is in certain songs and artists that yields global superstardom vs others who might actually be better at the craft.
label: question
notes: Asking whether successful artists actively seek fame or whether fame can happen unintentionally through their music.

text: I  think they all chase fame. But there‚Äôs examples where, once that fame happens, they quickly realise it‚Äôs not really what they wanted after all. An example would be Remy Shand. Mutli-instrumentalist, records top seller, wins four Grammys, then just quits the industry entirely.
label: opinion
notes: Believes most artists pursue fame but argues that some later realize fame is not what they truly wanted.

text: A producer named Burial has avoided the trappings of fame as much as possible, UK newspapers went on the hunt to find out who he was. He doesn't perform live at all, has only been pictured a few times and is suspected to be around the frame of an early James Blake DJ set for Boiler Room and that's as much as anybody sees of him.
label: argument
notes: Provides Burial as an example to support the claim that some artists avoid fame despite achieving significant success.

text:
Gotye was an indie artist just trying to make a popular indie track that would maybe get a few plays on the triple J countdown or something. He was not expecting what actually happened.

If I‚Äôm not mistaken, he basically disbanded/stopped going by the name Gotye, and only releases the kind of stuff he was always releasing, under a different name to a fairly small audience now. I think the dude just never had any intention of being anything other than an Indie artist, but he wound up over shooting his goal.
