For what it‚Äôs worth I feel I should point out that the album ‚Äúsomebody that I used to know‚Äù was on, was actually quite a good album.
label: argument
notes: Uses Gotye as an example to support the idea that some artists become famous unintentionally and never sought mainstream success.

text:
Syd Barrett - just seemed to want to make eclectic weirdness. Had no idea how to handle major fame.

The band he was in became on of the most successful in the history of the music biz (Dark Side of the Moon spent 743 weeks on the billboard 200 list).

Just sort of collapsed under the pressure.
label: argument
notes: Uses Syd Barrett as an example to support the idea that some musicians did not seek fame and struggled when it arrived.

text: Ben Howard isn‚Äôt exactly super popular but he was after his first album. He clearly didn‚Äôt want to be that famous and spent the next few albums trying to shed those fans I think. Seems to just be into making the music he wants and not interested in being Ed Sheeran like people wanted I think.
label: argument
notes: Uses Ben Howard as an example to support the idea that some artists prioritize artistic expression over maintaining mainstream fame.

text:
Syd Barrett literally had a mental breakdown over his desire to prioritize art over commercialism. People solely blame the drugs, but he genuinely lost interest in fame. It wasn‚Äôt what he expected it to be.

His problematic behavior in Pink Floyd was both a mix of rebellious behavior (e.g. refusing to lipsync on pop shows) and drug-induced catatonia. But people tend to only focus on the latter.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Syd Barrett's struggles were partly driven by his rejection of fame and commercial success, not just drug use

text:
Old proggers will remember a character called Mike Oldfield, who was super-successful in the 70s, and at one point had albums at Number 1 and 2 in the UK charts.

He was more of a thing in the UK and Europe. He never broke the US.

But he was the absolute definition of an extreme introvert, possibly autistic, then his first album turned into a best seller, some of the music was used in a popular movie, and he absolutely refused to tour or do any promotions of any kind - except for one live performance, for which he demanded Richard Branson's Rolls Royce, because he was sure he wouldn't get it. (He did.)

Instead of he locked himself in a remote cottage in Wales, went for walks in the hills, and recorded another album.

He eventually did a therapy course and turned into a slightly weird semi-extrovert. (Also discovered coke, which likely helped.)

None of this seems to have been a manufactured pose. He was never as fragile as Syd Barrett, and is clearly a much tougher character. But for a good few years he was the definitive "Shy sensitive musician suddenly becomes famous and hates it."
label: argument
notes: Uses Mike Oldfield as an example to support the idea that some musicians achieve major success without wanting fame and actively avoid the spotlight.

text:
Better‚Äù is subjective however you look at it. And as a songwriter, you usually do want people to enjoy what they‚Äôre hearing. Personal fulfillment along the way can be good, but almost all songwriters will never be 100 percent happy with the stuff they write anyway. That‚Äôs how it is for most creative types. And if you‚Äôre in a position with some success, having people respond to your stuff would probably be a hard thing to give up. Or just say ‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôm done with that, let‚Äôs get the audience cut down to a quarter of what it was and avoid all nice-listening hooks now!‚Äù

Overall. I‚Äôd say if you have heard of any major act, you probably are listening to someone that has a stake in having the public like what they‚Äôre hearing hear. Or had their material pushed on MTV, VH1, radio, SiriusXM, Rolling Stone, Blender, or what have you. If it‚Äôs only about the ‚Äúlove of music‚Äù or wanting to push boundaries (whatever that may mean), you might be better served reading music bios on Bandcamp for bands that have a few dozen plays, or hanging out in dive bars with musicians that also load and unload their own equipment, and so forth.
label: argument
notes: Argues that most successful musicians want an audience and that widespread recognition usually reflects some desire for public attention and success.

text:
Great description and overview! I think there's a lot of nuances to fame discussion but it gets boiled down to "Chasing Fame vs Doing it for the love of music" as a straightforward dichotomy.

To build off your point about wanting people to enjoy what you're hearing: one could argue that it's almost a base human desire to want to connect with others with your art. Humans are social animals after all. Even some of the most challenging artists at least wanted to get an emotional reaction out of their audience.

Popular artists is certainly the key phrase. As you say, most of the major artists we hear about wanted some level of fame. It could be a niche audience or it could be global popularity. But they likely want some resonance with their audience.

I'm wondering if you've read the SPIN Alternative Record Guide. It's an interesting time capsule of that Gen X alternative ideology. Some of their picks are strange and don't make sense but the basic idea is that they focused on artists who avoided mass culture. So Elvis, The Beatles, and The Rolling Stones, no matter how rebellious or experimental they were, were impacting mass culture by virtue of being big. Whereas the Velvet Underground's influence was comparatively built more subtly and over time.

A number of artists they list don't even fit the criteria they're describing. But it shows just how prevalent that type of thinking was.

you might be better served reading music bios on Bandcamp for bands that have a few dozen plays, or hanging out in dive bars with musicians that also load and unload their own equipment, and so forth.

I laughed a bit at this last part, sounds pretty apt. But it does make me reflect on how discovery of obscure music has changed in method and in status. I think it used to be a sign of taste to dig for obscure artists. While it still is to some extent, it's both more accessible but also hard to discover things that are genuinely obscure.
label: opinion
notes: Discusses different perspectives on fame, artistic expression, and music discovery while agreeing with and expanding on another person's viewpoint.

text:
Harry Nilsson.

He was one of those "your artists favourite artist types" I think they called him the American beatle at one point. His biggest hit is a cover of someone else's song, which is ironic becasue a few of his own songs ended up as huge hits as covers

His writing isn't for everyone as he's an extremely self aware and goofy character. But he has one of the most beautiful voices you'll hear and when you pair that with his whimsical stylings you get gold.

Basically he never performed live because he hated it all. If you go and listen to his stuff you'll either know it or you'll know the cover version of it. 

Moonbeam is still probably one of my favourite songs of all time.
label: argument
notes: Uses Harry Nilsson as an example to support the idea that some successful musicians achieved fame despite having little interest in performing or being famous.

text:
This was how most R&B artists operated until the crossover era. They primarily stuck to their market of a black audience and whatever mainstream appeal they got was just icing on the cake. They didn't "avoid" the mainstream but they didn't alter their sound for it. It wasn't until the days of Michael Jackson having those mega crossover hits that people started to see the money and fame you could get from that and started chasing it, some more successful than others.

Otherwise I can think of Prince as someone who is/was very famous but was always conscious to still perform in his own way. Especially something like how he handled Purple Rain really blowing up, Around The World in a Day is almost like actively defusing the rocket and later on something like Parade is an outright rejection; if you're going to hop on the Prince train it's going to be conducted by him on the journey that he wants.
label: argument
notes: Argues that many artists, such as Prince and earlier R&B musicians, prioritized artistic control over maximizing mainstream fame.

text:
Were Jimmy Cauty and Alex Paterson ambient music‚Äôs great lost partnership?
Since discovering Jimmy Cauty‚Äôs Space, I‚Äôve become fascinated by the incredibly brief creative partnership between Cauty and Alex Paterson.


It feels as though something special happened whenever these two crossed paths.


They were the original incarnation of The Orb. Together they developed those early ambient DJ sets, worked on ‚ÄúA Huge Ever Growing Pulsating Brain That Rules from the Centre of the Ultraworld,‚Äù and began the project that eventually became Space before their partnership fell apart.


Then they separated, and this extraordinary cluster of music emerged around them: Space, Chill Out and The Orb‚Äôs Adventures Beyond the Ultraworld.


Obviously, the precise credits and chronology are complicated. Paterson has long claimed that he contributed significantly to Chill Out (on one podcast he claimed it was 80% him!), while Space was reportedly stripped of his contributions and reworked by Cauty following their split. There are competing accounts, bruised feelings and probably no completely objective version of what happened.


But I‚Äôm less interested in figuring out who made what percentage of each record than I am in the creative relationship itself.


Listening to the original ‚ÄúPulsating Brain,‚Äù Space, Chill Out and the early Orb material, I keep wondering whether Cauty and Paterson brought something unique out of each other.


Perhaps Paterson supplied the endless curiosity, samples, strange juxtapositions and free-associative flow, while Cauty brought a particular sense of shape, atmosphere and conceptual scale. That is just interpretation, but together they seemed capable of making music that felt enormous, playful, mysterious and completely outside ordinary time.


Both men made brilliant work without each other, so this isn‚Äôt about diminishing either of them. But that short overlap feels almost alchemical. It helped establish a musical language that both The Orb and the KLF would then carry in different directions.


Had they stayed together longer, would The Orb have become what it became? Would Chill Out exist in the same form? Would Space now be regarded as an ambient classic rather than an obscure KLF-adjacent curiosity?


Or was the brevity and tension of their partnership exactly why that music was so special?


I‚Äôd love to hear from people who know this period better. What do you think each of them brought to the partnership, and where can you hear it most clearly?
label: question
notes: Asking whether the partnership between Jimmy Cauty and Alex Paterson was uniquely influential in shaping early ambient music and what each contributed creatively.

text: Alex Paterson has built a career out of partnering up with people with actual talent.
label: opinion
notes: Expresses a negative opinion that Alex Paterson's success largely came from collaborating with talented partners.

text:
What makes an album a "grower" rather than an instant favorite?
Some albums make an immediate impact and are easy to connect with from the very first listen. Others can take weeks, months, or even years before their strengths become apparent. Whether it's unconventional songwriting, complex production, unfamiliar genres, or simply hearing the music at the right moment in life, certain records seem to reward patience in a way that others don't.

What do you think separates a "grower" album from an instant favorite? Is it something in the music itself, the listener's expectations, or the context in which the album is heard? And do you think albums that require more time and effort to appreciate tend to leave a stronger long-term impression?
label: question
notes: Asking why some albums take time to appreciate and what factors make them "growers" rather than instant favorites.

text: a grower album is usually just something your brain cannot map on first listen. it feels flat because the patterns are not obvious yet. after a few repeats your mind starts predicting it better and then it clicks instant favorites are usually just familiar structure from the start. nothing to decode so it lands right away context matters more than people think. your mood and attention level changes what details you even notice. an album can feel boring one day and perfect the next just because you are in a different headspace most people never give albums enough time anymore. they skip around playlists on spotify or apple music and move on too fast for anything to really grow when you actually sit with a record it changes. repetition builds attachment and you start hearing layers you missed before some people use platforms like arena and even r/ GetArenaRadio which pushes full listens with that listen to earn setup. tidal and soundcloud still feel more instant reaction based so growers are less common there in the end grower albums usually stick harder because they feel earned instead of instantly given
label: argument
notes: Argues that grower albums require repeated listening because familiarity, context, and deeper understanding reveal qualities that are not obvious at first.

text: Getting Killed by Geese was one for me. First time I ever heard their music was on SNL, and they did ‚ÄúTHERES A BOMB IN MY CAR.‚Äù I sort of rolled my eyes and found them annoying. But the SNL subreddit was talking about what a great band they are. So I listened to the album on spotify and it didn‚Äôt really click. I gave it a few more months and listened again and ended up getting used to the musical and vocal style and enjoying it quite a bit.
label: opinion
notes: Shares a personal experience of an album that became enjoyable only after multiple listens and greater familiarity with the band's style.

text:
Insane Clown Posse is actually kind of underrated.
Insane Clown Posse might be the most hated music group in modern history. More than Nickelback, more than Imagine Dragons, more than Limp Bizkit, more than any of them. Unlike those 3, ICP NEVER had support from media, and their fans were designated a gang.

But even though musically the group is certainly not everyone's cup of tea, they are honestly a lot better than people give them credit for.

They get judged harshly because they're not "serious" rappers- what the hell do you expect from guys who dress up in clown makeup and spray Faygo over their audience? Illmatic Part 2?

But they are great storytellers and have a solid sense of humor. Some songs I recommend are:

"12" from Riddle Box. This song is a great concept song. It‚Äôs about a jury of 12 and a guy who was sentenced to death by them for a crime he didn‚Äôt commit. So he comes back to kill all 12 that condemned him.

"The Pendulum's Promise" from Bizaar. This song is about people judging others, and don't realize... they are also in many ways describing themselves.

"Crossing Thy Bridge" from The Wraith: Shangri La. This is borderline metal. It's about being horrified at seeing children die at a young age, and talking to God about wanting redemption.

"Halls Of Illusions" from The Great Milenko. Probably one of the closest things these guys have to a "hit". A great concept song about taking child abusers and woman beaters on a deadly ride representing a fun-house to reflect upon their actions, as they are shown the consequences of their ways as they pass through the ‚ÄúHalls of Illusions‚Äù.

If you want to check out those songs, do so and give ICP a chance. If you just blindly go by the "ICP sucks" mentality that you're supposed to think because Eminem famously dissed them and Miracles went viral... more power to you.

You're not gonna want to drink Faygo and paint your face up after listening to these tracks, but it could give you a solid look at the other side of the clowns.
label: argument
notes: Defends Insane Clown Posse as underrated by highlighting their storytelling, creativity, and the unfair criticism they often receive.

text: ICP and their juggalo fanbase has been reevaluated by critics and the mainstream in general in the last ten years or so. The general consensus has shifted from them being low brow culture vultures to inclusive outsiders. Even people who don't like their music admit that they are good people.
label: argument
notes: Argues that public perception of ICP and Juggalos has improved, citing a broader reevaluation by critics and mainstream audiences

text:
Why is London Calling so Popular?
Typically, if an album is ranked among the greatest of all time, I try to listen to it until I understand why people view it that way. However, in the case of London Calling by The Clash, I have spent hours listening to it (mostly the first half) and I don't really get the appeal at all.

The highlights for me are primarily in the punk-y songs including the title track (a masterpiece), Clampdown, and Death or Glory.

Brand New Cadillac is fine but I'm not sure how this version substantially improves upon the Vince Taylor version. The Reggae/Ska tracks seem to substitute the original rhythmic of the genres with a fairly traditional rock beat that doesn't really fit. I thought most of the other tracks were generic new wave or pop rock songs paired with Joe Strummer's mediocre singing voice.

Many reviews rave about the "primal energy" of the album yet it seems like many new wave artists had already combined punk energy with musical sophistication by late 1979. Furthermore, IMO the instrumentation is some of dullest I've seen from an all time great album and nothing really packs a punch.

If anyone could help me understand what makes London Calling so great, I would be very appreciative.
label: question
notes: Asks why London Calling is considered a great album and seeks explanations for its critical acclaim.

text:
It's always like this when people can't get why an old Album seem very popular, most simply can't get that it influenced everything that came out after it.

I really dig their sound and it was a gateway for Ska and Rocksteady, which later introduced me to Jungle and DnB so it continues to influence me musically even 20 years after I got my Album.

I could go on to why I still find it a Masterpiece but I recommend OP trying to understand the Clash's own references that influenced their sound., it's all there. No one sounded like that in the 70s.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the album's reputation comes partly from its historical influence and originality, supporting the claim with context about its impact and musical references.

text:
Thoughts on Moby criticizing the Kinks' song Lola?
The Guardian just released an interview with Moby that includes this:

The song I can no longer listen to
Lola by the Kinks came up on a Spotify playlist, and I thought the lyrics were ross and transphobic. I like their early music, but I was really taken aback at how unevolved the lyrics are.

https://www.theguardian.com/music/2026/mar/22/moby-honest-playlist-donna-summer-celine-dion?utm_source=firefox-newtab-en-us

My first thought, as somebody who loved Moby in the 90s, is that he's virtue signalling as a way to distract from his long history of being accused of sexual misconduct and attending Diddy parties.

My second thought is, "Wait, "Lola" is transphobic?" I've always viewed it the opposite way: He loves a cross-dressing man or a transwoman (it doesn't say which), and Lola is the only one who has it together ("It's a mixed up muddled up shook up world except for Lola").

My third thought is, "What kind of hack journalist is interviewing Moby and doesn't take the opportunity to ask him about Diddy's parties?"

What are your thoughts on all this?

Are any of you still listening to Play? It was my favorite album when it came out, but I've found it no longer hits me the same way. I'm not really into EDM, but when I want serious thinking/studying 90s EDM, I put on the Chemical Brothers, and when I want silly 90s EDM, I put on Fat Boy Slim, and Moby doesn't really scratch either itch for me anymore.
label: question
notes: Asks for opinions on Moby's criticism of "Lola" and raises several discussion points about the song, Moby, and his music.

text:
WTF is Moby even on? For the time when it was written ìLolaî was extremely progressive. Sure, thereís a moment in the song where he pushes her away perhaps in revulsion - though that is unstated and only one interpretation - but it winds up with him on his knees in front of her and ìThat's the way that I want it to stay. And I always want it to be that way for my Lolaî

I could see a transphobic person looking at the song as a transphobic nightmare but that would be projection. Thereís zero in the song that verbalizes anything against Lola.

It does refer to her as a man, but Lola was likely a Transvestite as Transexuals were nearly non-existent at the time from a cultural standpoint. And the general public wasnít even introduced to ideas of dead-naming and misgendering for another 30 years.

And even if there could be problematic angles to Lola that I fail to see, the song is from the 70s. I think you have to take a piece of art in context of the era when it was made. Judging whether the lyrics of a 70s song are evolved by todayís standards is like asking an artist to see the future every time they make something.

ìHey MOBY if youíre so progressive, why did you do a collab with Gwen Stefani when sheís a MAGA sellout?î
label: argument
notes: Argues that "Lola" was progressive for its time and that judging it solely by modern standards ignores its historical context and actual lyrics.

text:
Love them or hate them, the #1 most "American" band of all time is the Grateful Dead. Fight me.
Their sound is a melting pot of strictly American genres with an improvisational approach akin to what some deem to be America's only original genre - Jazz.

If the American Dream dictates that if you work hard enough you'll make it, this band toured more than most in human history.

Money Money: Just as a touring band they made more $$ than almost any other in history.

- ^^ They've generated $1 billion + in merch by allowing fans to brand the band for them.

If you think I'm wrong think again because I'm totally right about this one folks...Name me a more American band, I dare ya. Tell me how this isn't true.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the Grateful Dead are the most "American" band by citing their musical influences, touring history, commercial success, and cultural impact.

text: I wonít fight you, say your peace and get out ???I love the Dead donít get me wrong, but there are a lot of groups who could fit the most ìAmericanî band of all time bill. There are numerous famous bands with a melting pot of strictly American genres and have lived the American dream by working hard until theyíve made it. While the Deadís touring record is certainly unmatched, I wouldnít say that itís a determining factor in how American of a band they are. Same with the amount of revenue theyíve generated. I think they can definitely proclaim being ìONE OFî the most American bands of all time, but to be so narrowed minded to think the Dead is the only one is ignorant IMO. That being said, there are none other like the Grateful Dead and they are probably by far the most successful unique band of all time and have created a whole culture all by themselves.
label: argument
notes: Argues that while the Grateful Dead are highly representative of American music and culture, they are not uniquely the most American band because other bands could also fit that description.

text: I think one big thing that sets the Dead apart, and makes them more of a candidate for "most American" is how deliberate and self-aware they were about leaning into Americana and the whole vibe and mythos of it all. Other bands and artists of course played with Americana styles, but the Dead were like Americana on steroids (well, on acid lol but you get my meaning).
label: argument
notes: Argues that the Grateful Dead stand out as a candidate for the most American band because they consciously embraced and amplified Americana themes, imagery, and culture.

text:
If, as you claim, jazz is the only fully American genre, surely a jazz band would be the most American band.

Regarding the American Dream, bandleader Art Blakey is truly the epitome of it. He was descended from African-Americans who had migrated from the south to the north in search of a better life. His single mother died when he was little and he was subsequently raised by various family friends. He was self-taught and ended up being a highly respected drummer and the leader of one of the most seminal jazz bands of all time.

Influence. During the bandís 35 years of existence, with Blakey as its only fixed member, it was a training ground for many young musicians who went on to become legends. Some examples are Wayne Shorter, Donald Byrd, Jackie McLean, Lee Morgan, Hank Mobley, Freddie Hubbard, Keith Jarrett, Chick Correa, Kevin Eubanks, and brothers Wynton and Branford Marsalis.
label: argument
notes: Argues that a jazz band could be considered more American than the Grateful Dead, using Art Blakey's life story, influence, and contributions to jazz as supporting evidence.

text:
The band that arguably originated the genre now called "Americana" was... The Band, with their debut album "Music from Big Pink" (1968) and the self-titled follow-up (1969, which contained "The night they drove old Dixie down"). And they were four-fifths Canadian, including their main songwriter.

The Dead's classic Americana albums, "Workingman's Dead" and "American Beauty", came out in 1970. Their earlier work leaned more to psychedelia (originating in Ken Kesey's acid tests, which is equally American of course).
label: argument
notes: Argues against the Grateful Dead being uniquely the most American band by pointing to The Band's role in shaping Americana music and providing historical evidence.

text:
James Brown has a strong argument for being the most influential figure in popular music and doesn't get enough credit for it
For starters, as the primary innovator of funk music, he immediately influenced artists like Parliament-Funkadelic and Sly and the Family Stone, and even the likes of Miles Davis. Even non-western genres like Afrobeat are directly inspired by Brown and Fela Kuti himself studied Brown's rhythms to create the genre. He's also one of the major innovators of soul music (along with Ray Charles and Sam Cooke). His rhythmic innovations on funk also directly led to disco, and subsequently other genres like EDM. He can also be argued as the godfather of hip-hop, as he's the most sampled artist ever and early hip-hop in particular was heavily built on his music.

Not to mention as a performer he influenced numerous rock frontmen. Mick Jagger and the Rolling Stones famously were nervous following his performance and Jagger admitted he stole his moves to become a more charismatic frontman. David Bowie was also influenced by his showmanship and one of his biggest hits, Fame, is directly influenced by James Brown's sound.

Michael Jackson's biggest influence was James Brown and you can see the latter's fingerprints all over Michael's style, from his rhythmic vocal style to his dance moves. Prince similarly took a lot from James Brown as well.

There are many who compete for the title, including Louis Armstrong, The Beatles, Phil Spector, Bob Dylan, etc. but in terms of everything from musical output, to influencing so many genres, to influencing performance styles, James Brown seems to be in a league of his own.
label: argument
notes: Argues that James Brown is one of the most influential figures in popular music by citing his impact on multiple genres, artists, and performance styles.

text:
My big recommendation for people with James Brown is the 1991 compilation Star Time. You can find it on streaming services. Itís quite extensive. Nearly 5 hours of music, its contents span most of the length of his career up to the time of its release, starting in 1956 with his first hit record, "Please, Please, Please", and ending with "Unity", his 1984 collaboration with Afrika Bambaataa. Not that you should try to listen to that all in one sitting, but if you sample it I think it makes the best case overall for why James Brown was such an amazing artist. It was compiled by people who really knew what they were doing.

Writing in 2007, Robert Christgau described it as "the finest box set ever released... as essential a package as the biz has ever hawked, not just because it's James Brown, but because compilers Cliff White and Harry Weinger invested so much care and knowledge in it." Its title comes from the question Brown's announcer would ask concert audiences, as heard on the 1963 album Live at the Apollo: "Are you ready for star time?"

In 2003, the album was ranked number 79 on Rolling Stone magazine's list of the 500 greatest albums of all time. It was the second-highest ranking box set on the list. In 2012, it moved up to 75, while in its 2020 revision, it moved to number 54.
label: argument
notes: Supports James Brown's importance by recommending the Star Time compilation and citing expert reviews, rankings, and its comprehensive coverage of his career.

text:
Yeah he absolutely backed up the moniker of ìthe hardest working man in show businessî. His band was also second to none, being funky yet had a tightness and precision that was unparalleled.

However, as innovative as he was, thereís no question he was abusive and a terrible person to his bands and partners. But looking at his upbringing, being abandoned by his mother, raised as an orphan by his aunt in a goddamn brothel, it doesnít excuse his difficulties but adds some context
label: argument
notes: Argues that James Brown's extraordinary work ethic and musical innovation should be acknowledged while also recognizing that his abusive behavior can be partially understood through the context of his difficult upbringing.

text:
Look up what he did in Boston on the night MLK was killed. He kept the town from melting down.

Of all the dumb BS he pulled, he made up for it by literally saving lives that night.

He was questionable in many respects, but itís also worth noting that of all the hard starts in life, JB had one of the hardest. He wasnít born into a situation where he could be a good dude.
label: argument
notes: Argues that James Brown's positive actions, particularly helping prevent unrest after MLK's assassination, and his difficult upbringing provide important context when evaluating his character.

text:
Randy Newman is the greatest living songwriter whose body of work is practically unknown by the general public
If you asked an average person on the street who is Randy Newman you would probably get one of two responses at a push three responses

Who?

The Toy Story/Pixar guy?

Short people guy?

This is a guy who has only had one top 40 hit in his entire career and yet is a critical darling and highly respected amongst his peers Bob Dylan once called him "The king of the mountain."

This is someone who has never put out a clunker of an album like Dylan or McCartney has done. Music, lyrics, arranging he can do it all. Sail Away, Good Old Boys, 12 Songs are some of the best albums of all time. Indeed his last album released in 2017 shows no decline in his abilities.

So why hasn't he been successful commercially outside of film work, on his studio albums which is where you find his best work?

There a couple of reasons:

The subject matter of his songs; he can write love songs with the best of them see Feels Like Home or She Chose Me but these are rare anomalies as the former type of songs doesn't interest him much.

He chooses to write songs with a subject matter that isn't commercial such as God's contempt for mankind or an advertising jingle by a slaver trying to encourage Africans to sail away with him to a mythical promised land. The music itself is commercial the lyrics are anything but.

2. His looks and aura. He comes across as more a college professor than he does a rock star. There have never been hordes of girls chasing after him like the Beatles because he was good looking. While this shouldn't matter it does hurt you commercially if you're not conventionally attractive or have an aura like Lou Reed.

3. His singing voice once described as a "startled bison" turns a lot of people off as well. Being not the best singer in the world doesn't stop you being a megastar see Bruce Springsteen or Bob Dylan but it can hurt you if paired with the above two other factors.

A combination of all these 3 make him the greatest living songwriter largely known by the general public
label: argument
notes: Argues that Randy Newman is the greatest living songwriter largely unknown to the general public, supporting the claim with evidence about his songwriting quality, critical acclaim, and reasons for limited commercial success.

text:
He's a great songwriter. To your list, I would add a few more reasons why much of his music is no longer well known.

He's a man behind a piano. This is much harder for a popular musician to pull off successfully. Elton John and Lady Gaga do it with outrageous costumes and big choreographed production. That's not his style.

He's a victim of his success as a Hollywood composer. Beginning in the eighties, he started to put more time and effort into his film scores than his solo music. When Toy Story came out, he became so strongly associated with the soundtrack that it became his signature, especially with audiences who hadn't grown up with his songs in the seventies. When you keep getting Oscar nominations for Best Song for Pixar films, it's hard to continue to draw interest from people who are not part of that demographic.

It's not just that he writes songs about difficult topics. It's that many of his songs are delivered ironically ("Let's drop the big one and see what happens"). It goes over the heads of many listeners, especially those who are looking for something they can relate and sing along to.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Randy Newman's limited mainstream recognition can be explained by his performance style, film-composer success, and the ironic, challenging nature of his songwriting.

text:
I'm not sure that even if he wasn't behind a piano that Mr Newman would be the most dynamic of performers.

With regards to his Hollywood success that probably increased people's general awareness of him before then he was probably known for just one novelty hit short people.

What it do though is dramatically decrease the rate at which he puts out studio albums. I'm hopeful we'll get one more studio album out of Randy and if we do that will almost certainly be the last one. If we dont then Dark Matter is a fine album to end a decades long career on.
label: argument
notes: Expands on the discussion by arguing that Randy Newman's film success increased public awareness but reduced his studio output, while also commenting on his performance style and legacy.

text:
Your comment makes me think of this scene from the Keira Knightley film Begin Again.

https://youtu.be/NTp2jIiSrbA?si=t_pZsm3mbI8Ao_yb

But yeah, even his probably most well known song, outside of Toy Story, I Love LA is pretty subversive in a very clever way. He never beats you over the head with anything.

But back to the clip above. I generally do not like when people talk about authenticity in music, or literature, or even politics. Ultimately, I have no idea if someone is inauthentic, but skilled at selling an image and skilled at writing, or if someone is authentic, but awkwardness or clumsy writing makes them come across otherwise.

But my dad, who has now passed away, was very good friends with Lyle Lovett who told me that there really is not artifice with Newman. He just is who he is. It is not that he rejects fame, it is just something that does not interest him.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Randy Newman's authenticity and lack of interest in fame help explain both his unique artistic reputation and his limited mainstream celebrity status.

text:
I was so stoked to see this post. I 100% agree with you. Heís my favorite songwriter of all time. No one can make you laugh and then cry on the same album more than he can. Sometimes within the same song.

I also donít think any musician has written about America as effectively as he has. He uses all that Tin Pan Alley, blues, and Dixieland vocabulary to make his songs sound like they could be national anthems, and then he sticks in the knife about our hypocrisy. But it never feels nasty, itís almost like some sugar to make the medicine go down that weíve never been who we think we are.

Sail Away the song is the prime example of that to me, but there are so many others. Sigmund Freudís Impersonation of Albert Einstein in America is another deep cut that comes to mind. But sometimes, theyíre genuinely sweet too. Dayton, Ohio - 1903 comes to mind. Louisiana 1927 makes me tear up pretty much every time I hear it.

Anyway, Iím glad thereís another member of the Randy Newman Appreciation Society out there! There arenít many of us ??. Idk if youíve ever seen the video where heís playing I Want You to Hurt Like I Do and is telling the crowd about how itís his version of We Are the World, but that one is so funny. A Wedding in Cherokee County, My Life Is Good, and The Great Nations of Europe always kill me too.
label: opinion
notes: Expresses strong admiration for Randy Newman as a songwriter and describes the emotional impact and themes of his music.

text:
I donít quite understand the hype for Geese - Getting Killed
Iíve been looking for great new rock music for a while so I was excited when I heard about Getting Killed. So I listened to it andÖit was okay? I guess?

I can tell theres talent behind it and it is pretty unique music but when I listened to it I felt absolutely no emotion. All the spastic and loud instrumentals felt purposeless. Like they did it for the sake of it. The vocals felt like someone putting on a voice, not speaking from their soul.

Is it something I need to see live to understand? Do I need to be in the right mood? I want to understand it and love it but I just donít.
label: question
notes: Asking why Getting Killed by Geese receives so much praise and whether there is something about the album that the listener is missing.

text:
The less I try to make myself like ìthe thingî the less I hate ìthe thingî.

Itís a massive improvement in my life. Other people are allowed to vocally actively love things I donít know anything about and even if I check it out and just donít get it at all, itís fine.

I think thatís basically how people have been making friends all these years lmao
label: opinion
notes: Expresses the view that it's okay not to enjoy popular or highly praised things and that people should not force themselves to like them.

text:
The main thing you need to understand is that sometimes an album becomes really popular that is not loved by literally everyone that hears it.

Back in the day when Smells Like Teen Spirit was released I had exactly the same conversations with my friends.

It certainly makes it easier to not like an album when most of your friends, or the content creators you follow, also don't like it. But when everyone raves about something it feels weird to not like it, you need to just be confident in your own taste.

Also fwiw I prefer 3D Country to this album. I like the more experimental elements and the intensity. This album seems too normal to me. But I am happy for them.
label: argument
notes: Argues that not every critically acclaimed album will appeal to everyone and encourages trusting personal taste rather than feeling pressured by popularity or consensus.

text: Iíd rather listen to Ween, Beck, Tom Waits, Zep, Zappa, King Gizzard, Sparklehorse, The Beatles, Radiohead, Blur, basically any other cool and weird rock shit over this album. No idea what the hype is about. They had a good performance of Trinidad on Youtube but I have similar complaints with others in this thread. Instrumental is fine if a bit busy but nothing I havenít heard before and the vocals are completely lacking in emotion and there are sections where theyíre made cuts in vocal takes that are really obvious and I find personally distasteful. That said, thereís plenty of worse shit to listen to, but I didnít find anything particularly innovative on the record. Plenty of new artists releasing music every day doing shit youíve never conceived of
label: opinion
notes: Expresses personal dislike of the album, comparing it unfavorably to other artists and arguing that it lacks innovation and emotional impact.

text: Iím with you. Havenít liked any of their stuff. I find the vocals to be annoying and on the verge of insufferable. There is nothing exceptionally impressive from a melodic standpoint, and I canít understand why so many have gravitated to this band. Oh well, at least people are appreciating music with actual instruments again.
label: opinion
notes: Expresses personal dislike of the band, criticizing the vocals and melodies while acknowledging that people are enjoying instrument-based music.

text: I won't bend over backwards to defend this record, although I love it, because I think you just have your own preferences and you definitely have a right to that. I will say I don't find a chief criticism here very compelling at all. Reading your comment, it signifies a pretty narrow view of art, one that prioritizes only genuine expressions. I think there are other means of artistic expression or creativity. I don't think putting on a voice (to use your specific point), adopting a persona, speaking in abstractions, etc. are inherently bad. These things can convey themes from different angles, not just through transparency or vulnerability. Should I reflexively discount Bowie's entire discography because various albums were created to express different personas? Because he was putting on another voice for the whole runtime? I don't want to be overly critical here. Just putting out some food for thought. I think others may approach the record differently than you and can enjoy it in different ways than you are searching for.
label: argument
notes: Argues that artistic value is not limited to direct emotional authenticity and that techniques like personas, altered voices, and abstraction can be valid forms of artistic expression.

text:
I think I've seen Geese mentioned as a rock band, but I had never heard their music until now.

Having listened to this song a few times now, I don't find it particularly grabbing or eliciting much emotion from me. The singer's voice doesn't really work for me, and at times there is this shrillness to it which, while not as bad as Thom Yorke, doesn't sound good to me. The music behind the singer lacks memorable hooks, and it doesn't manage to create much of a mood, just rumbling along there.
label: opinion
notes: Shares a personal reaction to Geese's music, criticizing the vocals, lack of memorable hooks, and limited emotional impact.

text:
I donít get it either. Itís borderline unlistenable for me, he drones over everything.

I get that everyone has different tastes and I can imagine there are people that like this album, but it seems pretty niche and I canít understand how itís as big as it is. Itís on everyoneís best of year list but if I put this on in front of friends or family theyíd be like what the fuck.

Itís not just this either, loads of critically acclaimed stuff from recent years sounds terrible to me. Loads of dense, miserable, ugly music. Last year it was that Geordie Greep album.
label: opinion
notes: Expresses strong personal dislike of Geese and other recent critically acclaimed music, finding it difficult to understand their popularity and praise.

text: I feel like somebody at a record label somewhere really fucking wants me to love it because I canít take a shit without someone tapping me on my shoulder going ìhere mate, you heard this geese record?î Itís on everyone and his dogís year-end lists. It was Bandcamp album of the week at one point. I keep seeing the cover art everywhere. Even got recommended it by a guy at work. Out of a sense of stubbornness, I tend to reflexively want to avoid any product which is being actively pushed on me, but I finally gave in and listened yesterday.
label: opinion
notes: Expresses frustration with the amount of promotion and attention Geese receives and describes a personal reaction to finally listening to the album.

text:
This 1000%, idk whose dad bought the record label or what dicks they sucked but every possible media outlet is shoving this band in my face constantly, like even Spotify is having their songs pop up when I search for totally unrelated things. Reddit is suggesting me the geese subreddit etc etc it's everywehere.

The mad thing is I gave them a listen on a few occasions, checked out the album, and also tried to watch a vid of a live show, and oh my god it's so insanely mid. Bland noise, totally forgettable, no real melody or interest. They seem kinda bored? Lyrics (when you can even hear them) are meaningless drivel, poorly sung/moaned. Nothing interesting happening with any of the instruments either, and it's not like with some bands where it's more than the sum of its parts and simple stuff sounds amazing, its a sum of shit parts and it sounds like shit? Like I'm pretty sure the average high school has multiple better bands made up of stoned 16 year olds.

The upside is that im sure some great new bands are gonna come thru soon because the bar seems to be incredibly low.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Geese's popularity is driven by excessive promotion rather than musical quality, supporting the claim with examples of media exposure and criticisms of the band's music.

text:
"I donít quite understand the hype for..."

people to this day will say this about Talking Heads, Velvet Underground, Animal Collective, REM, Joy Division, Radiohead, Tom Waits... every band, especially alternative bands, will have people who don't like them, it's fine, you have no obligations to enjoy everything.
label: argument
notes: Argues that disagreement about acclaimed artists is normal and uses examples of well-known alternative bands to support the point that popularity does not require universal appeal.

text: Huh! Interesting. Especially the vocals because I feel the complete opposite way and heís said in interviews that heís not putting on a voice and very much trying to sing from a place of his own, unique voice and not some sort of ìtrained singerî but more like a Leonard Cohen or Tom Waits (Tho arguably Tom does put on different character voices lol) and to me the spastic instrumentals are really cool and indicative of all the bandís varied influences and to get a bit more conceptual, itís of the current culture in music where people are listening to tracks mostly and from tons of different genres and time periods and to me the music captures some of that lack of monoculture and blending of the past and current music into a whole smorgasbord!
label: argument
notes: Argues that Geese's vocals and instrumentation are authentic and meaningful, supporting the view with references to the singer's intentions and the band's blend of diverse musical influences.

text:
So is music for the rich now?
Just saw Rick Beatoís YouTube on musicians mostly coming from wealthy families these days and wondering what folks think. I do remember music seeming to come from more lower and middle class kids in the 60s up thru 2010 but Iím less in tune with that these days. Do musicians practice in garages and try to get local gigs any more? I think traditional route was to play a lot of smaller local bars, get noticed by an agent and then get a record deal. Does everyone just record at home now and go straight to the internet? Iíd think a more organic route would be to play live as much as possible but maybe thatís old school these days. Could there be a revival of that and music coming from any demographic?
label: question
notes: Asks whether music has become dominated by people from wealthy backgrounds and how musicians typically build careers in the modern music industry.

text:
If it's anything like the film industry (my line of work), you can be the most amazing writer/director ever, but if you don't have the connections to funding and famous people, you can't really get far. I know so many talented filmmakers who've given up or left LA or are toiling away as assistants to the rich and famous.

Not sure about the music industry, but it does feel like things are getting more and more like that throughout all sectors.
label: argument
notes: Argues by analogy to the film industry that talent alone is often insufficient for success, and that connections, funding, and access increasingly determine opportunities.

text:
It's not just music. Fewer and fewer people can own a home; you can't have a garage band if you don't have a garage. Developing your art takes time, and if you have to work three jobs just to pay your rent, that's time you're not spending on playing or writing.

Producer Joe Boyd says as much in his memoir, White Bicycles:

The atmosphere in which music flourished then had a lot to do with economics. It was a time of unprecedented prosperity. People are supposedly wealthier now, yet most feel they havenít enough money and time is at an even greater premiumÖ. In the sixties, we had surpluses of both money and time.

Friends of mine lived comfortably in Greenwich Village, Harvard Square, Bayswater, Santa Monica and on the Left Bank and were, by current standards, broke. Yet they survived easily on occasional coffee-house gigs or part-time work. Today, urbanites must feverishly maximize their economic potential just to maintain a small flat in Hoboken, Somerville, Hackney, Korea Town or Belleville. The economy of the sixties cut us a lot of slack, leaving time to travel, take drugs, write songs and rethink the universe.
label: argument
notes: Argues that economic conditions make it harder for working- and middle-class people to pursue music, supporting the claim with examples about housing costs, work demands, and a quotation from Joe Boyd.

text: Forget about owning a house and working, go back a little bit to teenagers. A guitar (or other instrument) is expensive. Leaning to play one takes time. It used to be that the neighborhood kids would get bored and go find each other to kill time, play sports, maybe start a band. Now thereís no boredom. If you have nothing to do, you can turn on your tv or phone and instantly find something to consume your time. Why learn to play a physical instrument when you can do it on a laptop quicker and easier? I still believe that the best musical innovation comes from boredom and drugs.
label: argument
notes: Argues that modern technology, reduced boredom, and the cost and time required to learn instruments make it less likely for young people to form bands and develop musical skills.

text:
It's always been like this but it's worse now because the capital that drives the industry has concentrated a bit further and is now firmly in the tech industry.

The thing is...that shouldn't stop you from making music and enjoying that process. It might change the ways we we engage with that process but so long as I have time I will continue to experiment with sound simply for the joy of doing so. I'll likely never be profitable, it will likely cost me money...but I will keep making and releasing music for as long as I can.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the music industry has become more concentrated and difficult to succeed in, but maintains that people can still create music for personal fulfillment regardless of profitability.

text: Iím glad youíre doing it too. There was an interesting interview I heard about how the UK managed to support ìstarvingî musicians during its wildly creative period. Seems like there were some lessons in there.
label: opinion
notes: Expresses interest in how the UK supported struggling musicians during a creative period and agrees with the idea of continuing to make music despite challenges.

text: While there have always been busking troubadours and folk and tavern performers, most of the professional arts have always largely come from relatively affluent people - there's just a lot of cost involved in having the exposure to art, the time to develop artistic skills, advanced training in those skills, the materials and housing costs of the medium, access to display/performance spaces and other markets, etc.
label: argument
notes: Argues that professional success in the arts has historically been easier for affluent people because artistic development requires time, resources, training, and access to opportunities.

text:
Add to that how the record labels screw over new artist with lousy contracts.
Streaming music is also a problem, which generates minimal profits.

Bad Bunny was poor and made music in his bedroom that he posted online and word got around. That's the new template for up and coming artist. It's tough out there.
label: argument
notes: Argues that financial barriers, unfair record contracts, and low streaming revenue make it difficult for new artists to succeed, while citing Bad Bunny as an example of a modern path to success.

text:
To answer your question, yes musicians do still practice in garages and try to get local gigs. If you are in a big city in the US, that is very much still alive. Probably on a much smaller scale then previous eras however. But it is not dead.

Two things to add to what others have said:

There are straight up fewer local gigs and venues. I'm in NYC and it's very rich with local venues and bars and young bands all about the music life. But I grew up in the Bay Area in the 90s, and those places have gradually become much fewer and further between. Bar gigs also tend to have a lot of older bands, probably reflecting who has time, money and the inclination to play out in a band and to see one. Gen Z is notoriously bar (and outside) adverse. The Bottom of the Hill closing this year will be the end of the last of the truly great local band venues in San Francisco. SF is much more dominated by DJs and DJ venues now.

The children of the wealthy have always dominated arts but there used to be a much clearer delineation between high culture and music and vernacular or popular music. Fifty years ago and wealthy son would be more likely to be an cellist than a DJ. Popular music was the music of the populi, the people, the unwashed masses. Now not so much. Pop music and pop adjacent styles have become much more interesting to and embraced by the elite and some of that is reflected in the musicians now.
label: argument
notes: Argues that local music scenes still exist but are smaller than before, and explains changes in venues, demographics, and the increasing role of wealth in popular music.

text: Yeah same kind of shrinking going on in my city as well. Fewer kids going out, fewer small gigs. More empty venues. Crazy because clubs and venues had lines going out the door at 1am 15 years ago. Pandemic?  Smartphones? Streaming?  I think all of the above but places used to be packed and partying until 3am. This during the Clinton years so early internet boom, but man what a nosedive since 2015.
label: argument
notes: Argues that nightlife and local music scenes have declined over time and suggests possible causes such as the pandemic, smartphones, streaming, and changing social habits.

text: There are plenty of musicians of all genres playing locally everyday here in Midwest. For $10 or less (free) you can catch some wildly talented musicians playing jazz, country, djís whatever suits you. Support your local musicians in this world when concert tickets are run by greed and bots. Find your next fav
label: argument
notes: Argues that vibrant local music scenes still exist, encourages supporting local musicians, and contrasts affordable local shows with expensive mainstream concerts.

text:
I would argue the contrary. Its never been cheaper to actually put together something of reasonable audio quality yourself. Browse souncloud and there are tons and tons of new music being put out everyday.

Now actually having music as your main profession (which i think i what Beato is getting at) is a thing for the rich, but that's more because music as a whole does not pay as well as it used to. Labels aren't investing in 10 new artists/bands to go record hoping 1 of them is good. Usually now its the opposite, you have to release stuff, show you have a following and then you get signed.
label: argument
notes: Argues that making and distributing music is more accessible and affordable than ever, but earning a living from music is harder because the industry now expects artists to build an audience before receiving label support.

text: They do where I live. We've got a bunch of local bands here, but this is also a city with a long musical history at this point. Even beyond regular local venues there's a free summer concert series that features a bunch of local talent, some folks were having shows under a bridge last year, there's house shows, twice yearly arts festival with local bands, etc.
label: argument
notes: Argues against the idea that local music scenes are disappearing by providing examples of active local bands, venues, festivals, house shows, and community events.

text:
Not necessarily for the rich, but the industry has abandoned anything that requires money and/or development time.

A music industry executive explained it to me like this: ìWhy would I sign a promising band, which is expensive, time consuming and requires me to pay multiple people when I can sign a young solo artist who has already hit the algorithm, requires only studio musicians (or AI), has a built-in social media audience and is willing to do exactly what we ask of them without committee?î

Iím fairly confident that in five years half of the music we hear will be AI generated.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the music industry now favors inexpensive, low-risk artists over developing bands, using an industry executive's explanation as evidence and predicting increased AI-generated music.

text:
I was in a lot of bands that didn't go anywhere and one reason was economics. You can't even practice at home anymore in many places and have to rent a space. Music equipment is expensive. And people with jobs can't practice that much.

A bunch of working class guys can't just drop everything and do this all day. It was about 2010 when I stopped for good. Bands that "made it" were bands that had a rich kid in them or connections. They had better equipment, time to practice, time to record and money to record. They could promote their shows. Now I could afford all this shit, but have zero time.

My daughter's fiance was in a band that toured and because they don't come from wealth, it was basically three guys living in a van, camping in parks. He plays local shows now, but the band thing is pretty much dead and they opened for big acts, released music on their own.

This problem is not new. It will be worse with a shit economy. You either have to be so desperately broke and unemployable that you may as well go for it like the Ramones and The Damned did or be a bunch of rich kids like The Strokes. Hell, people forget that the main reason Jerry Only and Doyle were originally in the Misfits is because they were rich kids compared to Danzig.

There is a space in the middle for being in a band that just plays local gigs when you can, but that will probably never get you beyond your local scene. Sick of it All is perhaps one of the greatest success stories of a working class band that broke out, but that's not common.
label: argument
notes: Argues that economic barriers make it difficult for working-class musicians to succeed, using personal experience and examples of bands with wealth or connections having advantages in equipment, time, promotion, and touring.

text: I have seen a similar question in startups in coding and other industries. One important point is that if you are poor, you can only fail once. It takes all of your resources to try to launch a company, a band, or an art career. If you are from a well off family, you can fail multiple times because you have family resources and a support network to pick you up again. So the odds have always favored artists and entrepreneurs who come from the upper end of the economic spectrum.
label: argument
notes: Argues that people from wealthier backgrounds have an advantage in creative fields because they can afford multiple failures, while poorer individuals often have only one chance to take a major risk.

text:
Honestly this is survivorship bias all the way down. Art has always been a rich kid game, going back centuries. Mozart's dad was a court musician. Chopin grew up around Polish aristocracy. The impressionists mostly had family money or patrons. The romantic image of the working-class musician kicking down the door is real for a small window, mostly post-war America and Britain, but it was never the historical norm. It was the exception that everyone now mistakes for the rule.

The reason you remember more lower/middle class musicians from the 60s-2010 era is that you saw the ones who made it. You didn't see the thousands of poor kids who picked up a guitar at 16, played in garages for three years, ran out of money or had a kid or got a real job at 24, and disappeared. Meanwhile the rich kid down the street kept going for another decade because his parents could float him. It's not that wealthy families produce more talent. They produce more endurance. The runway is the entire game.

And here's the part that's actually counterintuitive ó the technical barrier to making music has collapsed. A teenager with a $400 laptop and a free DAW can make something that would've required a $200K studio in 1985. By every objective measure poor kids have more access to music production than at any point in human history. So why does it feel like rich kids dominate? Because the bottleneck moved. It's no longer "can you afford the equipment", it's "can you afford to do this for ten years without it paying you". And that part hasn't changed. Probably got worse, since the streaming economy basically guarantees you'll be broke for longer.

I'd push back on the Rick Beato framing a bit. When he says musicians come from wealthy families now, what he's really observing is that the people who can survive the music industry's economic structure long enough to become visible are disproportionately those with safety nets. That was always true. We just had a brief romantic period where it didn't feel that way. Anyway you'd need an actual large-sample study to know what's really going on, and I don't think anyone's done it. Everything we're saying including this comment is just looking at the tip of the iceberg.
label: argument
notes: Argues that wealthy people have historically had advantages in the arts, explains why success appears more common among rich artists today, and supports the claim with historical examples and reasoning about economic barriers.

text: What no one is discussing is that wealth is needed these days to make any kind of headway in streaming numbers and/or social media recognition. It's the dirty secret of modern music. There is a lot of talk about "Anyone with a laptop can make music, post it online, and grow a following on social media". However, like payola before it, there are now gatekeepers with a network of industry contacts that pitch your songs on streaming to playlists, Youtube, etc. that help get more ears and eyes on your music that otherwise would have never found it. Money is needed for that and it is not cheap. There are gatekeepers that are paid to be considered for certain awards, tours, venues. Also, money is needed in order to boost social media posts and create ads. Could all of this happen organically? Sure, but it rarely does.
label: argument
notes: Argues that financial resources are increasingly necessary for success in modern music because streaming promotion, playlist placement, advertising, industry connections, and social media visibility often depend on money rather than purely organic discovery.

text:
Albums used to make money, hence record deals. Now it kind of sounds like you're on your own and it's just a creative project, like tending flowers or something, gardening on your property. The whole industry was a cottage with songwriters and cream rising to the top or one group having an outstanding effort. I guess there's no money in that anymore, so it's all manufactured with the lowest costs possible. This will probably be hard for people to hear if they didn't realize music took a nosedive after downloading.

Add to that streaming which is all paid click farms for profit and divvying up the proceeds amongst the artists who can afford to do that, it's all a con game basically. The album culture was the peak of commercial music and we've fallen hard since then, besides a couple indie labels.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the music industry's decline in album revenue, the rise of downloading and streaming, and the influence of paid promotion have reduced artistic development and shifted music toward lower-cost, manufactured production.

text:
Rick Beato thinks that what he hears on mainstream radio is the extent of the music world.

He has no idea about grassroots underground scenes.

Music making technology has never been as affordable and widely available to the masses as it is today.
label: argument
notes: Argues that music creation is more accessible than ever and criticizes Rick Beato's view by claiming he overlooks thriving grassroots and underground music scenes.

text:
Is Hendrix getting lost in time?
When I was in high school a lot of kids, usually the stoner types were big into classic rock. And the teen stoner starter kit for classic rock was primarily Floyd, Zeppelin, The Stones, and Hendrix. These days I have heard a lot of the younger generation still digging all of these, except not much about Hendrix in recent times. I know his estate did not permit his music on streaming platforms for a while, so that might be a big part of it. Am I wrong, or does Hendrix not seem as prevalent as a pillar of classic rock like he once was?
label: question
notes: Asks whether Jimi Hendrix is becoming less culturally prominent among younger generations and explores possible reasons for the change.

text:
And maybe a lot of this is more people arguing as to why they should be remembered and not so much as to why they think they will, which is a difference in interpretation I guess.

Because I can sit here and argue all day that the kinks are one of the most important bands in 60s rock ëní roll and their continued relevance throughout the 70s along with the fact that some of their best work contains very little in the way of hits and that Ray Davies is one of the best British songwriters of the 20th centuryÖ. But the fact of the matter are that they are already, while still known and discussed in some way, a footnote of a memory in terms of your general American person who listens to music who is in their 20s or so. They might remember you really got me if you played it for them they might know all day in all of the night, but otherwise they wonít factor them in their life whatsoever.
label: argument
notes: Argues that there is a difference between an artist deserving to be remembered and actually remaining culturally relevant, using The Kinks as an example of a historically important band whose recognition has faded among younger listeners.

text:
I canít believe people are so dismissive of him here. Yes, he is famous for being good at guitar. But nobody cares about Hendrix because they think he can play notes fast. Have you heard Are You Experienced, The Wind Cries Mary, Little Wing, 1983, Voodoo Child, Machine Gun, and so many others!

These are incredible songs with amazing lyrics and melodies that stick with you, the songs are why people care. Not because he can ìshredî. Of course itís true that as rock declines he will be a part of that decline, but itís insane to me to say that he doesnít matter anymore because some random dude on YouTube or something can play faster.

Maybe, but can some random shredder write an actual song with an actual melody? Doubtful!
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix's importance comes from his songwriting, melodies, and musical creativity rather than just technical guitar skill, using examples of his songs as evidence.

text: I agree with this. And some songs in his catalog are very accessible as well: Purple Haze, Angel, Hey Joe, Voodoo Child. But then it strikes me that his voice might work against him in an age where carbon copy American idol contestants can get recognition for their classically trained voices over unconventional artists who have a unique sound and style. Jimi made due with the voice he had, and often used a sing-talk style that might also turn off some younger listeners
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix's music remains accessible and suggests that his unconventional vocal style may make him less appealing to some modern listeners despite the strength of his songs.

text: I think heís still apart of the pantheon in a big way. I have noticed a trend where it seems like 60s artists arenít as recognized anymore, I donít think The Doors, Janis, Jefferson Airplane, or The Byrds are as culturally relevant as they were 30 years ago. Hendrix I feel like will always be a mainstay because of how he revolutionized guitar playing. As far as 60s bands go I think only The Beatles, The Stones, and maybe The Grateful Dead will have the same relevance as he does
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix remains a major figure in rock history and supports this by comparing his lasting influence to other 1960s artists whose cultural relevance has declined more significantly.

text:
One thing Iíve noticed, is heís not embraced by mainstream Black culture as other iconic Black artists, such as Prince or Stevie Wonder or MJ. You seldom see him mentioned during Black History month, despite the fact that he is probably one of the most influential Black musician of the century.

I read an interesting article a few years ago about how Jimi himself was upset that he wasnít received as warmly in Black music circles during his life, and it seems that continues. Iíd venture that this has something to do with his fading popularity.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix's relative lack of recognition within mainstream Black culture may contribute to his declining visibility, supporting the claim with observations and references to Hendrix's own concerns during his lifetime.

text:
I think it has to do with the decline of rock. If rock is no longer relevant, neither are its iconic figures.

For example, new R&B is "in the game" now, and I think that in some ways that has benefited R&B artists like Marvin Gaye, who was brilliant and was somewhat overshadowed by the whole rock phenomenon.

As for Hendrix, well, I agree with what I said: there isn't as much of a cult following for the '60s as there seemed to be until the early 2000s. Besides, his psychedelic blues-rock music is somewhat difficult for today's ears to grasp.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix's declining visibility is linked to the decline of rock music and changing musical tastes, using comparisons to R&B artists and shifts in cultural interest as evidence.

text:
My perception of where Hendrix has fit in popular culture is that he is a god, but a god bound to his time. His last living release came in early 1970, and he never achieved the same FM ubiquity as Zeppelin or Floyd, because FM radio was always 70ís centric. So, Hendrix is something of a ghost of the 60ís.

Without radio exposure, one has to be aware and curious to make a new discovery of an old artist. As public awareness of rockís history gets distilled into fewer and fewer bands, it gets harder to know that this is something one may like and seek out.

I see The Doors headed in this direction as well.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix's declining visibility is due to being strongly associated with the 1960s, receiving less long-term radio exposure than artists like Zeppelin or Pink Floyd, and becoming harder for younger listeners to discover.

text:
I feel like most of the 60s and 70s classic rock that millennials loved is getting lost in time. I think it's just the nature of how popular culture works. Combined with the overwhelming amount of content available now, I think it is just the natural evolution of these things.

The artist from that era that still survive and seem relevant are the ones who sell their shit at Walmart and Old Navy.
label: argument
notes: Argues that many classic rock artists from the 1960s and 1970s are fading from popular culture due to changing media consumption and the vast amount of modern content competing for attention.

text:
I think in the early 90ís, rock still ruled the airwaves, and when grunge game along born out of Seattle and guitar driven bands, there was a direct lineage to Hendrix. Add to that his influence on Prince, Lenny Kravitz and even in movies like Wayneís World, he was still everywhere in pop culture.

But thatís 30 years ago.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix remained culturally prominent through the early 1990s because rock music was dominant and newer artists and popular media maintained a connection to his influence, but that this relevance has diminished over time.

text: When my son turned about 14, his music listening went from hip hop/pop to classic rock bands like the Doors. I had a pretty crummy electric guitar and amp laying around and he started to play a bit. I remember walking past his room one day and he was trying to work out Oh Well. I clearly remember him asking me if I had heard of Jimi. Fast forward, heís in college now and when heís home, heís got a bunch of his boys over jamming to Machine Gun in our music room. Jimi was an early favorite of his, as he was mine. So no, heís still very relevant today to young music fans. Especially when they discover his music on their own.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix remains relevant to younger generations by providing a personal example of a son who independently discovered and embraced his music.

text:
I think there's some truth to this. Hendrix was a classic rock rite of passage for a certain kind of teenager in the 90's, but I don't get the impression that's the case so much now.

I think a large part of it is just that he's been dead a long time, his catalogue is small, and his sound and style is very much tied to the late 60's.

The 60's were back in fashion in the 90's, and his stuff fit quite well with all the alternative rock scenes going on at the time. Plus there were a bunch of archive releases, compilations and box sets coming out, which made him feel somewhat current even if he wasn't actually around. 30 years on, there isn't that much driving attention towards him.

All that said, his streaming numbers are still competitive with most of the other big 60's acts.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix is less prominent today because his music is closely tied to the 1960s, his catalog is limited, and cultural trends that renewed interest in him during the 1990s have faded, while noting that his streaming numbers remain strong.

text:
I feel like he will get to the point to where he is only known by serious musicians and academic types.

His career was relatively short and without a huge ton of material. He is more noted for his revolutionary techniques and innovations, and being the face of a counterculture, as opposed to making popular hits for the masses.

He is more akin to a famous scientist or inventor, or even more appropriately a classical era composer. People will know who he is by name, and maybe vaguely know his contributions and recognize a song or two.

But outside of academic or niche circles I think he will be mostly be known in passing knowledge or in referential terms, as opposed to having a large continuing fan base. He isnít like the Beatles or Rolling Stones where their pop appeal has kept them relevant in the modern day and still household names.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix's long-term legacy will resemble that of an influential historical figure whose innovations are remembered by musicians and scholars, while his broader popular appeal gradually fades.

text:
I think heís getting more into the historic side of things rather than getting more active listeners. I am 30 and remember being an ìold soulî for liking Hendrix & other music from the 60s-90s. Even all the dads of my friends were hair metal guysóMetallica & GnR were also huge in my area growing up.

I mean, the 60s were 60+ years ago. In the 90s and even early 2000s, that music was only 30-40 years ago (and most of that music was late 60s, so even more recent).

Woodstock happened roughly 25 years before Jerry died (ë95). Think of how different music and culture was 25 years ago. Now add 25 more years.

Soon, heíll turn into a Gershwin-type legend. People will perform lots of his music in music schools and artistic performances 50-75+ years from now and it will be considered in the ìclassicalî vein, in some way or another.

Lost in time? No. But times are changing and music sounds much different than it did 60-70 years ago. People still love it, just as people still love Miles Davis, Debussy, or Beethoven. It will always be remembered, played, and performed.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix is transitioning from a widely listened-to artist into a historical and cultural icon whose legacy will endure through education, performance, and influence, much like major classical composers.

text:
My perception is a bit skewed since I live in Haight-Ashbury. Yeah, Hendrix is still plastered all over the place here alongside Janis and the Dead.

As in there's literally a big mural of him on the side of a building, 1524 Haight St., just down a little bit from the intersection with Ashbury. Since painted red and promoted as the "Jimi Hendrix Red House" because he allegedly lived there for a few years. Which is apparently quite untrue.

But yeah, while he might not seem to be quite as big in the mainstream or the teenage classic rock starter pack, he's still at the forefront of "the '60s" as it's sold to people as a pre-packaged concept that doesn't really match reality.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix remains a central symbol of 1960s culture, citing local cultural visibility and the way the decade is marketed and remembered, even if his mainstream popularity has declined.

text:
To be fair it might be down to taste as well; I enjoy 3/4 of those bands and the one Iím not really a fan of is Hendrix. I know his cultural significance, and that people like them as much as I am aware of the cultural significance of bands like Beatles/Nirvana without me personally liking them.
