I do also think he is a lot bigger in the US than other countries due to Woodstock and all them festivals; hes often talked about as a brilliant guitarist but I donít know many (personally) who would stick him in the top 10 best bands/artists ever if they had to pick a list. And know itís not a definitive list but the Rolling Stones top 500 greatest albums has only 2 Hendrix on it (4 Floyd, 5 Zep, 6 stones). So a lot more are viewed by mainstream media to have more catalogue for people to go through.

I also tend to find that with the other 3 bands you have mentioned they are still a bit relevant in that all of them have members still alive so you hear about them a lot more, doing a tour or a reunion, solo albums etc.

Also you have to remember us talking about these bands in the 00s is like kids now talking about bands in the mid 80s to put it into context. Their classic rock has shifted a bit now too. Someone once told me they liked older rock like Blink 182 once as well.
label: argument
notes: Argues that Hendrix's perceived decline in popularity may be influenced by personal taste, differences between countries, a smaller catalog compared to other classic rock bands, and the fact that many contemporaries remain visible through surviving members and ongoing activity.

text:
Mot people who say they want "rock music back" don't seem to actually want new rock music
EDIT: whoops spelling error in the title. Pretend MOT stands for (music of thought or something)

i saw a post here a few days ago about how people "want rock music back in the mainstream", and while that may be true, it seems that today's rock audience doesn't want modern rock the way it is today.

to people I know who aren't big music listeners, specifically modern rock listeners, when it comes to modern rock, their tastes are often not all that modern. I'll hear they listen to newer bands like "Linkin Park" or "Foo Fighters", very evidently not the most "modern".

when it comes to actual new rock, the indie rock zeitgeist bands like Geese or Fontaines D.C. or whoever is getting today's indie love are barely heard of by anyone I know in real life. But there's almost always one band that comes up as the one band they know and like, which is Greta Van Fleet.

most who know of the band will know what will appeal to listeners most, and that's that GVF sounds a lot like Led Zeppelin. and while the band is talented, more than just watered down Zeppelin, there's little interest it would seem from people who complain about the lack of modern rock music to actually engage with modern rock. Other bands/Artists like Sleep Token & Maneskin make it mainstream by abiding to conventional modern pop sounds.

many rock bands who are pushed on social media are pushed not for their actual musical talent or the songs themselves, but often as "band x sounds like (old band)".

from what I've observed, when people say "I want to hear modern rock again", seldom does it actually mean wanting to engage with modern rock, but to hear modern rock that sounds like classic rock.

to people who lambast the lack of rock music in the mainstream, which is a valid point, the idea from many who say that is less about wanting to play the rock music that is popular and enjoyable, but rock that sounds like "the good old days". people don't want rock to be Geese or MJ Lenderman or BC;NR, whoever you want to throw out as "new rock", they want the next 'Rolling Stones', the next 'Led Zeppelin'.

it's no wonder that breakout bands like Greta Van Fleet are labeled as "the new Led Zeppelin' or Yungblud as "the next Ozzy". Rock can only exist in the mainstream acceptance as a form of nostalgia. any trending early 20's rock band will go ignored by the same people asking "where did all the rock go", but if you take a Stones cover band and ask AI to "generate a song that sounds like the Rolling Stones" and you'll have every person who can't name more than a handful of 21st century rock artists calling them "the last real rock band".

perhaps there's something to be said about the fact that "where have the rock bands gone" is almost always revolved around radio, which has become an increasingly older demographic of listeners, but if the public is wanting the return of rock music, they're doing a poor effort to find it themselves, and they don't seem to want to, because they want the nostalgia of classic rock to feel new again, for them to feel young.

I will note that from the LTM t head that OP however mentioned Spotify as a platform where rock is clearly popular, but that it's from pre-2000's bands such as Radiohead and Coldplay. Do the rock listeners even know that every music outlet has turned Cameron Winter into indie Jesus, or that big thief is supposed to be the coolest band of today.

even today the divide is so wide between the RYM/AOTY crowd that has declared Radiohead as the most genius band to ever live to the people making Radiohead trend on Spotify as 'oh, the band that made creep?" (do "rock fans" even know of 'The Smile'? their less than 500k monthly listeners answers that).

it's not that the indie crowd knows what is popular either. Geese has 1.9 monthly listeners. how many of these very cool hipsters have heard a single Sombr song, an artist who has 57M monthly listeners (and in all honestly isn't all that bad).

if people want to pay for new rock music, that doesn't track either with the billboard rock charts, where Queen's 'Greatest Hits', Nirvana's 'Nevermind', and Fleetwood Mac's 'Rumours' all lands in the top five, decades later. maybe the next big rock band is whichever classic rock band signs off their rights to become eternal AI slop.

constantly asking for "the return of rock bands" back, people don't actually want modern rock. what they want is a cover band with original songs.
label: argument
notes: Argues that many people who say they want rock music back in the mainstream are actually seeking nostalgia for classic rock rather than engaging with contemporary rock artists and scenes.

text:
I think there's a difference between saying you want new rock music and saying you want rock to be in the mainstream. There is an important social dimension to the latter. Bands like Geese and Fontaines DC are big, but they're not socially ubiquitous like pop or rap artists are these days. That's what people want: ubiquity. They want a rock band to be everywhere, played everywhere, a household name that is at the center of pop culture.

The irony is that there was a turning point at the height of rock music's relevance where the culture decided that they wanted the opposite of that: authenticity over trendiness, substance over flash. It used to be cool to know bands that nobody else knew, to be "alternative" or "underground", and accusing some band of "selling out" was a huge insult.

These things really do happen in cycles.
label: argument
notes: Argues that people who want rock in the mainstream are often seeking cultural ubiquity rather than simply new rock music, and explains how attitudes toward popularity and authenticity in rock have changed over time.

text:
Can't help but agree. Funny though that "classic rock" is an amalgam of "hard rock" bands who mostly riffed on blues music, and at the height of original popularity would tour with Stevie Wonder or Sly Stone or all kinds of music that doesn't fit the "classic" FM radio format. There was also a big "corporate rock sucks" which did kick back at Styx and Foreigner, Journey etc ..

I became a metalhead then punk rock kid early 80's then post punk etc (grunge wasn't rad at all to me after Green River)

Modern indie rock has 50 years more influences than just old blues or country. There's rad shit about disco, punk, metal, goth 80's, mainstream 80's, hip hop etc... Modern rock has sooo much more to be informed by and so many takes on Rock that are fresh and fun. I lean to more garage-y sounds but there's a whole universe of guitar oriented music today that's not painfully played out like Led Zeppelin, Pink Floyd and Quesn
label: argument
notes: Argues that modern rock is more diverse and creatively rich than classic rock because it draws from many additional influences beyond blues and country, and criticizes nostalgia-driven views of rock music.

text: I like interesting music and though I listen to a lot of 70ís and 90ís rock music, new bands that have interesting and unique sounds like Mortimer Nyx (newest that Iíve discovered, banger of a band that deserves way more attention), and King Gizzard (a wee bit older but also banger of a band) really offer something modern to the rock landscape. I think a lot of it has to do with gatekeeping rather than unwillingness.
label: argument
notes: Argues that there is still innovative modern rock music available and suggests that limited exposure or gatekeeping, rather than lack of quality, contributes to why more people do not engage with it.

text: People don't want rock. What they want is a new canon, to have huge rock anthems again. Itís not the same thing. There are plenty of great rock bands, but there haven't been any guitar hits anywhere for at least 15 or 20 years. People want the sound of what mainstream rock used to be again. 20-25 years ago you had huge hits by Green Day, Linkin Park, Chili Peppers, Muse, Foo Fighters, The Killers, Arctic Monkeys, etc. but you also had things like The Mars Volta, Postal Service, Arcade Fire, Animal Collective and a big indie scene. Now you have Geese, Black Country, New Road, Big Thief, Alvvays; but who is filling the void for huge rock songs? No one, and that is what people mean when they say "I want rock to be mainstream again."
label: argument
notes: Argues that people who say they want rock back in the mainstream are really asking for widely popular rock anthems and culturally dominant rock bands, not merely the existence of new rock music.

text:
A lot of people are talking about Rock nowadays. The problem is, they forgot about the Roll." - Keith Richards

Now there are some great modern rock bands sure, but many of these bands that call themselves "Rock" simply do not. Much of it feels like paint by numbers cosplay. The same recycled 4/4, always on the beat rhythms where the percussion and guitar tones feel sterile. There's little groove, or swing.

It's like, have people actually listened to Zeppelin? Because there's so much more to them than "Rock" and that's why they have endured for so long. Same deal with all these bands trying to copy Sabbath while leaving out all the cool stuff that made them special.
label: argument
notes: Argues that many modern rock bands imitate the surface elements of classic rock while missing important qualities such as groove, swing, and genre-blending influences that made bands like Led Zeppelin and Black Sabbath distinctive.

text: Anyone who says there isnít any good new rock music is simply lazy. All one has to do is seek it out. Go to some live shows, that opening band you never heard of might become your new favorite. Go to a music festival and check out the undercard acts. Even Spotifyís algorithm will often lead me to new bands I enjoy. If youíre stuck in the decades past wishing there was something new, venture out and find all the wonderful new sounds that might stretch your comfort zone.
label: argument
notes: Argues that good new rock music is readily available and that people who claim otherwise are not making an effort to discover it through live shows, festivals, or music recommendation tools.

text:
Most people who say the want ìreal rock music backî donít really follow new music. Itís the different between actively engaging in a scene and finding music you like, and being told what to listen to by the charts/algorithm. There are LOTS of great new rock bands out there. I live in a city that gets lots of live music, and I see great new acts several times a month at small-to-mid sized venues. Yes, some show influence from past acts (which is fine and natural when you work in a genre thatís been around for 70 years now), but most are doing their own thing and doing it in an exciting, fun way.

They arenít burning up the Hot 100, but I really donít care. Itís not stopping me from enjoying it.
label: argument
notes: Argues that people who want "real rock music back" often do not actively seek out new music, and supports the claim by pointing to thriving live music scenes and the existence of many innovative contemporary rock bands.

text: Yeah, half the people yelling for rock back want a comfort-food cover band for their teenage nostalgia. That Greta Van Fleet lane is the festival equivalent of booking a tribute act next to the main stage and acting surprised. What modern rock band actually lit you up the first time you heard them live, no classic-rock cheat code needed.
label: question
notes: Asks which modern rock band genuinely impressed listeners without relying on nostalgia or comparisons to classic rock bands

text:
What they want, in my opinion, is to recreate the experience of their youth when there were huge rock bands that had gigantic cultural presence, that their friends liked, that had big time shows that were exciting spectacles. They want to see people walking around with the T-shirts of the bands they like, they want to hear about their bands throwing things out of hotel windows, they want to see the singer with the it girl model or actress.

Back in the day, you really couldn't avoid listening to the bigger acts. This kind of sucked if, say, you didn't like Billy Joel or Def Leppard or whatever was currently on top. I felt victimized by that pap being constantly hurled in my direction whenever I was in a public place. There's nothing like that today; while I have heard Taylor Swift, it's pretty rare and I genuinely have never heard some of her most popular songs.

In other words, they want the monoculture to return, for their favorite rock band to be on top, and to be able to join a movement that feels important and can fulfill their social needs of belonging and giving meaning.

I would agree that the stated tastes of people who moan online about the state of rock do not seem particularly modern. Even in my local scene, the upcoming bands tend to feel like reworked aspects of bands from the 70s and 80s. It's rare that I hear something that makes my ears perk up.

That being said, Fontaines DC was all over the recent Peaky Blinders movie, and I went to see Caught Stealing half because IDLES did the soundtrack. Those two bands kick ass! Let's hope they can keep it up, and not lose what makes them awesome as (if) they become more popular.
label: argument
notes: Argues that many people who want rock to return are really longing for the cultural dominance and shared social experience of past rock eras, rather than simply seeking new rock music.

text:
I think a lot of Americans do not realize that modern rock bands do actually quite well in other countries.

Black Country New Road, IDLES, Fontaines DC, King Gizzard, Wet Leg, Big Thief, Yard Act etc... In America, these are seen as niche indie acts who almost never break into the top 40 charts. These artists are big, culturally relevant artists in the UK and Europe, and often are on the top 10, if not just straight up scoring a #1 on the charts. They are actually a part of the zeitgeist in a way they just are not in the US outside of cities like New York and Chicago.

I would argue rock is kind of dead in the US, but to an extent, so is everything. We are becoming a culturally barren country. Not even hip-hop is doing well. A very large portion of American youth have clocked out of engaging with modern music entirely. Outside of country music, the only real genre which truly has a zeitgeist is the big surge of superstar confessional pop artists like Sabrina Carpenter, Billie Eilish, Taylor Swift, and Olivia Rodrigo. But the fanbase of those artists is extremely demographically specific to young white women.
label: argument
notes: Argues that modern rock remains culturally significant in parts of Europe and the UK even if it has declined in prominence in the United States, and supports this claim with examples of successful contemporary rock bands and broader observations about American music culture.

text:
The inevitable end result of corporate media deregulation, sadly - monopolies, vertical integration, and consolidation. When I threw together a list of best films of 2025 there wasn't a single American film on there, and very little of the music I listen to now comes from the US.

Obviously there's scenes that have been exceptions, but, broadly, the US has always been hostile to art and intellectualism, and so it's cultivated incurious audiences which don't make for a market that creative music (or film) can flourish in.'
label: argument
notes: Argues that corporate media consolidation and cultural attitudes in the United States have reduced support for creative music and film, making it harder for innovative art to thrive.

text: I think what people are really saying is they want to feel the same way hearing "Smells Like Teen Spirit" when they were 15 made them feel. Which is never going to happen. And anyway, any genuinely new, groundbreaking rock music is going to piss off the olds. My dad was a huge rock guy, grew up with the Beatles, Stones, all of it. When grunge happened, he thought it was just a bunch of noise. Anything really, truly new likely won't even be a blip on the radar for people who just want the same but new.
label: argument
notes: Argues that people who want rock music back are often chasing the emotional impact of discovering music in their youth, and that truly innovative rock would likely be rejected by those seeking familiar nostalgia.

text:
Modern technology has changed the entertainment industry in general, not just music or rock / metal specifically. Rock / metal receding from mainstream is just one of many side effects of that bigger change.

Streaming (music, TV, Movies) all have seen the same decline in broader relevance. Even todayís pop stars are nowhere near as big or influential as artist of yesteryear. Why?

The ability to consume media ëOn Demandí with Youtube, Spotify, Netflix and the other varieties means that the audience is now splintered into 1 million small chunks.

In the past, a TV show would get 10s or hundreds of millions of regular viewers. Since there was only a handful of channels, most people were all consuming the same content. People would like that content to different extents, but everyone was exposed to the same thing. Not anymore.

You can have Taylor Swift or Ed Sheeren be the biggest music stars of the era, with number 1 positions on the charts, and millions of followers, yet millions of other people (likely over 50% of population) might not be able to name you a single song from these artists recent albums. Heck, I couldnít name you a number 1 song for ages because I, like most people these days, donít follow the charts anymore. We donít have to tune in on a Friday evening countdown to hear new music.

We all just listen to whatever music we want, at any time day or night. This is also why plays of old music is increasing while plays of new music is declining. Why listen to mediocre artist 157 when you could listen to Elvis perform one of the greatest songs of all time? Or, Why listen to generic rock band 237 who sounds like Linkin Park, when you can just listen to the best songs by Linkin Park? Its an inevitable choice.

This is a technology driven change to the market place. In the past there were thousands of artist selling millions of records. Now, there are millions of artists selling thousands of records.

Music production technology has also changed the nature of music away from bands and towards solo artists. What is a band and why have one? Its because you need multiple people who can play the different instruments. Both writing music and playing live. With todays computer software, you donít really record music, you simply use a computer to programme music. Theres existing library of all kinds of sounds from a single drum hit to symphony orchestras,. You could literally write and create a full symphony orchestra piece by yourself, working on a laptop at home.

That is how modern music is made because of its advantages. Ease, low cost, flexibility, musical freedom. The downside is everyone has access to the same tools, hence the seeming homogenization of musical output.

Itís the musical equivalent of having a unique hand crafted item of furniture back in the day, which could be expensive, flawed, but due to uniqueness, if well kept, may even appreciate in value overtime. Compared with, a perfect computer controlled factory made Ikia product thatís, quicker and lower cost to make. May be functionally better. However, because its mass produced, with 1000s other similar options, it has virtually no value.

In fact, the value of modern music is so low that its actually negative. Meaning that the artist now has to pay the audience to listen to their songs. This takes the form of paying to get added to certain playlists etc. This is just considered as a marketing expense. The hope is that people hear the song on a playlist, become a fan, and then pay to attend a live show, which is where the fans pockets will be emptied via overpriced tickets or merch items.
label: argument
notes: Argues that technological changes such as streaming, on-demand media, and digital music production have fragmented audiences, reduced the cultural dominance of artists and genres, and fundamentally changed how music is created, distributed, and monetized.

text:
Yeah I'm Gen X so my algorithm makes sure I run across these dipshits daily. They don't care about new rock music, they just want to complain about (entirely hypothetical, to them) new music and assume the music from when they were young is of course inherently better.

Most don't even care about the larper bands, they would just complain about it being unoriginal and their generation doing it first. It has nothing to do with music, it's about cultural identity and not forming opinions for yourself.

Honestly most of them are just racist and mad that there's too much rap and music influenced by rap. "Rap music? More like CRAP music!" Hold for applause
label: argument
notes: Argues that many people who complain about the state of modern rock are motivated more by nostalgia, cultural identity, and resistance to newer musical trends than by genuine engagement with contemporary rock music.

text:
You're blaming people for consuming what's out there, like they're the ones responsible for corrupting the entire system. Look at the telecommunications act of 1996, not Joe Listener. The entire ecosystem has been dismantled, and it's close to impossible for new bands to "break out."

Bands used to be able to get airplay by getting into the ears of certain DJs and getting traction in local markets, them using that success to bootstrap into expanding to other markets on the radio. Homogenization and corporate consolidation means you have one person programming dozens of stations now

I would love for more new music. But honestly in the last 10 years the only new artists I can remember hearing on mainstream rock stations are Highly Suspect and jelly roll. I couldn't get enough of Highly Suspect when I heard them, and couldn't switch off jelly roll fast enough.

Used to have luck discovering indie bands through some techniques using Internet radio to branch out, but that seems more difficult now given certain algorithm changes. Discovering new things is a lot more work than it used to be. Even college stations seem to have a lot less new artists
label: argument
notes: Argues that structural changes in the music industry, such as media consolidation and reduced opportunities for local radio exposure, have made it harder for new bands to gain attention and for listeners to discover new music.

text:
I can't speak for anyone else, and I don't demand that rock be mainstream. I just want good music, and I do miss rock. I just don't see a lot of stuff that is for me.

I like harder rock, and metal, but prefer to not have harsh vocals. This also eliminates a lot of the hardcore scene these days. A lot of the rest of hardcore is throwbacks that feel the need to have ass audio quality, and/or be relentlessly political in messaging. I like indie rock and all, but it's basically pop. No feedback, distortion, riffs, or punch. Pop punk used to be a lot heavier on the punk than pop in the 90s. Even 00s more emo-y pop punk was good. But now that's basically just Midwest emo which is more singer songwriter or pop. Regular pop punk is either gone or taken up by people like gags MGK.

Traditional hard rock & metal was good, but as you said, feels played out and derivative if new bands are reaching back to that. Grunge was good for when it lasted. Post grunge wasn't AS terrible as people now make it out to be, had some classics even, but definitely did heavily deteriorate into butt rock. There are people out there doing innovative stuff, but that's ultimately pretty weird sounding and experimental.

I know this makes me sound picky, and that's really not the case. I like just about every rock subgenre to some extent. Even the harsh vocals have a place. I think we have just settled too hard into the niches, eg indie rock has lost all edge and metal has too much edge.

There are always exceptions. Turnstile's Glow On album is like shoegaze + hardcore which ends up being s very nice balance of what I, and a lot of people liked pre-2010s while feeling completely fresh. I know reddit liking King Gizzard is a meme, but they are solid psychedelic garage-ish rock while still feeling fresh by adding microtonal sounds (think Indian music). They can feel floaty but have good energy. They even made a fairly traditional thrash metal album that doesn't feel trope-y at all, with an interesting album concept. It's not impossible to do, it's just not really the thing to do right now. And if you're going to put together a rock band, you usually have a specific plan in mind, and usually that's going to be a gatekeeper carrying the torch or someone doing weird shit to experiment.
label: argument
notes: Argues that many rock fans are not demanding mainstream success but are struggling to find modern music that matches their preferences, and supports this by discussing changes in rock subgenres, specific examples of bands, and broader trends in the genre.

text:
What many people long for is monoculture: one shared experience of the world that both unites citizens and offers a single script for social success.

They view classic rock as a symbol of that, a centerpiece of the last "shared" moment. Music stylistically resembling it suggests to them the possibility of a unified experience, and so they think that's what they want.

These audiences are wrong, of course. Classic rock was never monocultural, never ubiquitous. However, it was (and continued to be) framed that way because it had ubiquity among white menówho thrive on imagining themselves to be the central (indeed the only) players in culture.
label: argument
notes: Argues that nostalgia for classic rock is often tied to a desire for a shared cultural experience ("monoculture"), and further contends that perceptions of classic rock's cultural dominance are shaped by whose experiences were centered in mainstream culture.

text: It seems to be that A lot of people donít go looking for new music. They depend on social media to give them recommendations. I miss the days where the radio pointed us to new bands, and then browsing the cds helped to find new stuff. I still browse for new stuff, or set my music app to play lesser known bands.
label: argument
notes: Argues that many people rely too heavily on social media recommendations instead of actively discovering new music, and contrasts this with older methods such as radio and browsing music collections.

text:
I see your point. A lot of your argument is based on the assumption that rock has had its hey day and nowadays rock is just nostalgia in one disguise or another.

However, for example, when i listen to YUNGBLUDës ÑHello Heaven, Helloì i can identify elements that are genuinely modern. Towards the end, you hear him laying bare some facts about his creative process, going ÑYou reminded me / I tried to write this song / That was really fucking good / No synths at the endì. I feel like a lot of contemporary artistic efforts try to deconstruct the artist/listener boundary and open up more about their own vulnerability and insecurities, no longer claiming that dichotomy of ÑIëm the one that makes and youëre the one that consumesì, which I find is a very interesting development and can be traced across multiple current releases from different genres. Which all goes to say that there are still modern elements to rock music, itës not just nostalgia driven, imho. Times have changed and so have artistës sensibilities and the way they want to express their experieces made in the world of today.
label: argument
notes: Argues against the idea that modern rock is purely nostalgia-driven by pointing to contemporary artistic tendencies, such as increased vulnerability, self-reflection, and breaking down the barrier between artist and audience, using YUNGBLUD as an example.

text:
I see your point. A lot of your argument is based on the assumption that rock has had its hey day and nowadays rock is just nostalgia in one disguise or another.

However, for example, when i listen to YUNGBLUDës ÑHello Heaven, Helloì i can identify elements that are genuinely modern. Towards the end, you hear him laying bare some facts about his creative process, going ÑYou reminded me / I tried to write this song / That was really fucking good / No synths at the endì. I feel like a lot of contemporary artistic efforts try to deconstruct the artist/listener boundary and open up more about their own vulnerability and insecurities, no longer claiming that dichotomy of ÑIëm the one that makes and youëre the one that consumesì, which I find is a very interesting development and can be traced across multiple current releases from different genres. Which all goes to say that there are still modern elements to rock music, itës not just nostalgia driven, imho. Times have changed and so have artistës sensibilities and the way they want to express their experieces made in the world of today.
label: argument
notes: Argues that modern rock is not purely driven by nostalgia and supports this claim by highlighting contemporary themes such as vulnerability, artistic self-reflection, and the changing relationship between artists and audiences, using YUNGBLUD as an example.

text:
I think a lot of people want three things, which are unlikely to happen and are often incompatible with each other: they want rock to occupy a more central place in the culture like it used to; they want rock bands that feel aligned with the type of rock bands they used to love; and they donít want bands that are simply retreads.

There are newer bands out there playing more traditional rock music, but these bands are obviously not going to sound particularly current (their influences and musical touchstones are 40, 50 years old), and theyíre not likely to have much mainstream popularity. There are also newer, cult-popular bands that play variations of rock music that also include influences from other genres, but these bands are often going to be making music that purists might not recognize as ìrealî rock music. And then lastly, you have some extremely popular artists who arenít classified as rock who incorporate pretty obvious rock influences (Olivia Rodrigo being the most obvious example), but these artists donít look or act or feel like rock artists of old, to the point where people sometimes get offended when you suggest theyíre ìrock adjacentî or ìrock influenced.î

Thereís plenty of rock music being made still, but it requires a bit of open mindedness. ìHey, I like these guys, very old school, even though I canít see them ever getting a song on the radio.î ìHey, this band is doing something a little different, thatís interesting, maybe Iíll give them a chance and see if it grows on me.î ìYou know what, if I didnít know this was the new single from a big pop star, I would think this is actually a really solid ë90s style pop-rock song.î
label: argument
notes: Argues that people who want rock to return are often seeking a combination of mainstream relevance, familiarity, and originality that is difficult to achieve simultaneously, and explains how different modern artists and bands satisfy only some of those desires.

text:
The Beach Boys rightsholders have been releasing shady region-locked archive dumps digitally & de-listing them to retain copyright, now with intentional irreversible damage applied
Please pardon the rant-like tone, I'll try to be as concise with this as I can.

In 2011 the EU voted in favor of legislation that allowed the extension of copyright for sound recordings by 20 years on top of the original 50, with the stipulation that the recording must be made available within 50 years. This resulted in rightsholders of music by some major 60s bands (The Beatles included) rushing out shoddy releases digitally at the end of each year to retain the rights for another 20 years.

The Beach Boys camp have been releasing copyright dumps like this every year since 2013 (with some exclusions), as they are known for having tens of thousands of hours of unreleased audio in the archives. From around 2014 to 2018, these releases had thought and effort put into them as genuine archive releases, with brand new mixes and proper marketing, some even receiving physical releases! Fans were happy with this setup & the sets sold reasonably well.

Things took a turn, however, in 2019 when they were preparing the 1969-1971 Feel Flows box set for two of their most acclaimed albums, Sunflower and Surf's Up. That year, there were two digital releases: I'm Going Your Way, a 3-track EP of studio tracks that were eventually cut from the much-delayed Feel Flows set, and Live in 1970, a 3-hour 47-minute dump of live bootlegs ripped straight from fan uploads on YouTube (the first track genuinely starts with an ad that lasts 5 seconds before they click 'skip ad'). Both were de-listed a couple years later, unlike the earlier releases.

In 2021, they released a copyright dump of random studio & live recordings, as well as an entire unreleased album by The Flame produced under the Beach Boys' Brother label, on Bandcamp before deleting it, removing people's purchases without refunds. That would already be pretty bad IF EVERY SINGLE SONG WASN'T SLICED IN HALF!! Even the unreleased album, every track has the entire second half chopped off, leaving you only with the first few minutes of each song. Nothing from this dump has been released since.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the Beach Boys' rights holders have mishandled copyright-extension releases by issuing low-quality, temporary archive dumps, providing specific examples of poor practices such as bootleg sources, delisted releases, and intentionally truncated recordings.

text: Ugh that sucks, I was looking forward to the one this year (last yearís, which someone on here kindly shared with me via Google Drive, was actually a decent and included a version of ìBattle Hymn of the Republicî in better quality than whatís been circulating for years)! Thanks for the write-up though - would you happen to have a link to the full tracklist this year, and/or any other YouTube video links to the tracks?
label: question
notes: Asks for information about the Beach Boys copyright dump release, specifically requesting a tracklist and links to recordings.

text: Not so sure about the claim that there "tens of thousands of hours" of archival recordings. I mean, just 10,000 hours is equal to 416 days. Whatever is in the archives is likely mostly dreck & awful performances that shouldn't see the light of day. However, wouldn't it be amazing if there was a dedicated Beach Boys archivist (who was also a hardcore fan) who went through all those recordings & culled the absolute best, rarest, & most unusual music & conversations and regularly released them on a paid music service where fans old & new alike could enjoy them? They're such an incredible, influential group. IMO, they're the quintessential American band & Brian in particular was uniquely special. They deserve better than this.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the claim of "tens of thousands of hours" of archival recordings is likely exaggerated, and advocates for a curated archival approach where a knowledgeable archivist selects and releases the most valuable recordings for fans.

text: Funnily enough, around 2006 there was something similar to that in the works called Beach Boys Central, a website where fans could download unreleased music in a similar vein to the Neil Young Archives service. Of course that didnít come to fruition, so fans are left paying hundreds of dollars for individual songs from leakers. If they revived the idea now, I think it would be the best way to go about it for everyone. Oh well
label: argument
notes: Argues that reviving a centralized archive service for Beach Boys unreleased material would benefit fans, citing the failure of a similar past project and the current situation where fans pay high prices for leaked recordings.

text: It's a real middle finger to the fans. Like so many others, I was excited for the copyright release this year and it's just such a massive disappointment. What is the reasoning behind deliberately ruining the audio? Their scummy release methods have been bad enough, but at least those were still listenable!
label: question
notes: Asks why the Beach Boys rights holders would intentionally degrade or damage audio on copyright-protection releases and expresses frustration with the practice.

text:
Releasing that bitcrushed nonsense in an attempt to retain copyright is revolting behaviour. I can't imagine it holding up in court, though. Releases would have to be at least recognizable, which this arguably isn't. I'd love to see fans go up against Universal's fleet of lawyers: it'd make for a great indie film.

If they're looking to maintain the legacy of The Beach Boys, this isn't the way to do it.
label: argument
notes: Argues that intentionally degrading audio quality for copyright-retention purposes is unethical and potentially legally questionable, and contends that such practices harm rather than preserve The Beach Boys' legacy.

text:
Shame they choose to fuck with fans with rather poor mixing and EQing (looking at Linett's work around Feel Flows to right around Sail On Sailor 1972's release) and deliberately shitty moves such as the cutting songs in half part. (especially some of the songs Dennis had created, thankful for those who leak some of the best unreleased tracks)

It's clearly not a good move if they're going to try and market towards and keep a good reputation with both newer fans and old fans by doing this while also being painfully out of touch with what people want to hear and expect come with the new box set.

As I see it, it's somewhat similar with Brian's estate and their latest live re-release which is about the same but with the bonus tracks featuring fly-in vocals from Brian's first album. The fact there's even a small few people being paid to shill and to not specifically be too mean to either Brother Records or Brian's estate is a good example of staying far away from what some "social media influencer" has to say about a new product.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the Beach Boys organization has damaged its relationship with fans through poor audio decisions, questionable release practices, and a disconnect from what listeners actually want from archival releases and box sets.

text:
Lyrics are massively overvalued in how people judge music
I think music discussion puts too much weight on lyrics

Obviously lyrics can matter. A great lyric can make a song hit harder, and bad lyrics can ruin a song if they are distracting enough

But I think people often talk about music as if the lyrics are the ìrealî substance and everything else is just decoration. To me, that misses most of what music actually is

The melody, rhythm, vocal tone, production, dynamics, texture, atmosphere and structure usually do more emotional work than the literal words. A song can have very simple lyrics and still be powerful because of how it sounds. A song can also have clever lyrics and still be boring if the music underneath is flat

I also think this is why some people dismiss pop, dance music, metal, shoegaze, electronic music or music in languages they do not understand. They judge it too much like written poetry and not enough like sound

Lyrics are part of music, but they are not automatically the deepest or most important part

Do you think lyrics are genuinely central to judging music, or do music fans overrate them because words are easier to analyse than sound?
label: question
notes: Asks whether lyrics are overrated in music criticism and invites discussion about the relative importance of lyrics versus other musical elements such as melody, production, rhythm, and atmosphere.

text:
Yeah, I think thatís fair. I probably shouldíve separated listeners from critics more clearly

For casual listeners, lyrics might actually be undervalued a lot of the time. Plenty of people just want a catchy hook, beat, vibe, or something to play in the background

My issue is more with music criticism and certain online discussions where lyrics become the easiest shortcut for ìdepthî. But youíre right that in the wider population, a lot of people barely care what the lyrics are unless theyíre catchy or awful
label: argument
notes: Argues that lyrics may be undervalued by casual listeners but overemphasized in music criticism, where they are often treated as a shortcut for artistic depth compared to other musical elements.

text: My hook in to a particular song/piece of music is different. Sometimes its a layered production, sometimes lyrics, sometimes a bass line, sometimes some hazy guitar sounds. My ears just search for a sound i find agreeable. Im prob going to need better lyrics to carry say hip hop or folk than a house banger or some shoegaze where the lyrics are low in the mix.
label: argument
notes: Argues that the importance of lyrics depends on the genre and the listener's point of connection to the music, giving examples where production, instrumentation, or atmosphere matter more than lyrical content.

text:
In my experience, I'm one of the few people I know who pay any attention to lyrics at all. Most people are responding to the beat and the melody and if they know any line in the song, it's when the song title comes in on the chorus. I strongly tend to analyze lyrics as poetry, and the bands I love most are the ones who write wry and original lyrics, but that's unusual.

It may be that people who are into analyzing lyrics are drawn to become popular-music critics.
label: argument
notes: Argues that most listeners focus more on melody and rhythm than lyrics, while suggesting that people who pay close attention to lyrics may be disproportionately represented among music critics.

text: It's so bad, people use the words "music," song," and "lyrics" basically interchangeably and undervalue music. In another reddit post recently it was revealed that a songwriter was only writing lyrics and then using AI for the music, and half the comments were saying "why is everyone so mad? She wrote the music!" No she did not, it's surprising how many people seem not to realize these are two different skills, and the lyrics wouldn't feel half as impactful without the music. That's why we don't see spoken poetry streaming services with hundreds of millions of listeners.
label: argument
notes: Argues that people often conflate lyrics with music itself, undervaluing the musical component, and supports this claim with an example involving AI-generated music and the importance of musical composition in giving lyrics their impact.

text: How do you discover new music these days now that algorithms mostly recommend stuff that sounds like what you already listen to?
label: question
notes: Discussion prompt asking how others find new music
